speaker: Host
text_en: How has it created Ruby on Rails change how we build software now with AI agents? David Heinemeyer Hansen, often approaches DHH, created Ruby on Rails, Omatsji, and is a co-founder of 37signals.
text: Ruby on Rails 的诞生，如何改变了我们如今在 AI agents 时代构建软件的方式？David Heinemeyer Hansen，大家通常叫他 DHH，是 Ruby on Rails 和 Omatsji 的创造者，也是 37signals 的联合创始人。

speaker: Host
text_en: He bashed capabilities of AI coding tools on Let's Reasons podcast six months ago. Then over the course of a few weeks over the winter break, he did a 180 turn and went AI first on everything.
text: 六个月前，他还在 Let's Reasons 播客上猛烈抨击 AI 编码工具的能力。结果到了寒假那几周里，他来了个 180 度大转弯，几乎所有事情都改成了 AI first。

speaker: Host
text_en: In today's conversation, we cover how David and his team at 37signals build software today, and how AI tools are making them more ambitious than ever before.
text: 今天这期对话里，我们会聊 David 和他在 37signals 的团队如今是怎么做软件的，以及 AI 工具如何让他们比以往任何时候都更有野心。

speaker: Host
text_en: Why Ruby on Rails and Linux could become even more popular than they are today, as they are both well suited working with AI agents.
text: 我们还会聊，为什么 Ruby on Rails 和 Linux 可能会比今天更受欢迎，因为它们都非常适合和 AI agents 一起工作。

speaker: Host
text_en: Why taste and beautiful software are becoming more important, and why both standout designers and engineers who care about the craft could become more in demand. And many more.
text: 为什么品味和优美的软件变得更加重要，以及为什么那些出类拔萃的设计师和真正重视手艺的工程师，可能会变得更抢手。还有很多别的话题。

speaker: Host
text_en: If you're interested in what one of the most experienced builders in the tech industry thinks about the practical utility of AI tools and how these tools could impact software engineers who care about the craft, then this episode is for you.
text: 如果你想知道，科技行业里最资深的构建者之一，是怎么看 AI 工具的实际价值，以及这些工具会如何影响那些重视手艺的软件工程师，那这期节目就是为你准备的。

speaker: Host
text_en: This episode is presented by StatSig, the unified platform for flags, analytics, experiments, and more. Check out the show notes to learn more about them and our other seasonal sponsors, Sonar and WorkOS. David, it's awesome to have you here.
text: 本期节目由 StatSig 呈现，它是一个整合了 feature flags、分析、实验等能力的统一平台。想了解他们，以及我们本季的其他赞助商 Sonar 和 WorkOS，可以查看节目备注。David，很高兴你来到这里。

speaker: Host
text_en: Thanks for having me. Thanks for coming, I should actually say. You're in Copenhagen, that's my city of choice at the moment. It's a beautiful city. It's got so much going for it. And so what have you been up to? I'm always building stuff.
text: 谢谢邀请。其实应该说，谢谢你过来。你现在在 Copenhagen，那是我最近最喜欢的城市。它真的很美，而且各方面都很棒。所以你最近都在忙些什么？我一直都在做东西。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I have been building stuff for a good damn three decades now on the internet. I got started back in 94, I think it was, when I first got exposed to it and basically just never stopped.
text: 我在互联网上做东西，扎扎实实已经干了整整三十来年了。我大概是 94 年第一次接触到互联网，从那之后基本就没停过。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And in the past six months, I've been building a variety of things. One of them is a new Linux distribution called Umachi. I switched to Linux about a little over two years ago, I think now.
text: 而在过去六个月里，我一直在做各种各样的东西。其中一个是一个新的 Linux 发行版，叫 Umachi。我大概是在两年多前转到 Linux 的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: First spent some time on Ubuntu, having fun with that and then realizing I actually wanted to make my own system from scratch, building it on top of Arch and Hyperland. So I put a lot of time into Umachi.
text: 一开始我先在 Ubuntu 上折腾了一阵，玩得挺开心，后来又意识到，其实我想从零开始做一套属于自己的系统，于是就在 Arch 和 Hyperland 的基础上搭。所以我在 Umachi 上投入了很多时间。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It got started as a summer project in between racing at the 24 hours of Le Mans. There's a lot of downtime in that week. So I just started hacking on it and it really took off very quickly thereafter.
text: 它最初是我在参加 Le Mans 24 小时耐力赛期间，一个夏天的项目。那一周其实有很多空档时间，所以我就开始动手折腾它，之后它的发展速度真的非常快。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It's been a truly inspiring ride to see that even in a market as crowded as Linux distributions, there's about 7,000 different distributions out there.
text: 最让我受鼓舞的是，哪怕 Linux 发行版这个市场已经这么拥挤了，外面大概还有 7000 个不同的发行版。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Some of them with long pedigrees and many of them even based on sort of kind of similar vibes to some extent.
text: 其中有些历史非常悠久，而且很多在某种程度上，整体感觉其实也挺相似的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: There's room for something new and it's a great reminder that all the ideas in the world may be taken and it doesn't matter because your spin on it isn't.
text: 但新的东西依然有空间。这也很好地提醒了我：世界上所有点子可能早就都有人想过了，但那不重要，因为你的那种做法还没有。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And I put my spin on Linux, built Umachi, built the perfect computer system for me and saw exactly the same thing I've ever seen whenever I build something that really just hits the spot for me personally.
text: 于是我就把我自己的风格加进 Linux 里，做出了 Umachi，做出了对我来说最完美的电脑系统。结果我又一次看到了那个我每次做出真正戳中自己需求的东西时，都会看到的现象。

speaker: Guest
text_en: There are thousands of others just like me or close enough to what I like that they find the same pleasure and joy in it, whether it was Ruby on Rails, Kamal getting out of the cloud, any of these things. It's the same syndrome. Yeah.
text: 还有成千上万的人跟我一样，或者至少跟我的喜好足够接近，所以他们也能从这些东西里获得同样的乐趣和快乐。不管是 Ruby on Rails、Kamal 脱离云，还是这些事情里的任何一个，本质上都是同一种症状。对。

speaker: Host
text_en: With Rails, you were literally scratching your own itch. You were just building your own components and then open sourcing them. Is that how it started?
text: 说到 Rails，你当时真的是在解决自己的实际需求。你只是先做自己要用的组件，然后把它们开源了。事情一开始就是这样吗？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Basically, I picked up Ruby in the early 2000s and really put it to the test in 2003 when we started building Basecamp. And I did not have a mandate of what to use to build it.
text: 基本上是这样。我在 2000 年代初开始接触 Ruby，到了 2003 年我们开始做 Basecamp 的时候，才算是真正拿它来实战。而且当时并没有人规定我必须用什么来开发。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Prior to that, I'd been working for a lot of client projects that would say, well, we're building this in PHP because we have someone who knows that. So this is what you have to use. And then we were building our own system.
text: 在那之前，我做过很多客户项目，他们会说，嗯，我们这个要用 PHP 来做，因为我们有人懂这个。所以你就得用它。后来我们开始做自己的系统。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We were building Basecamp and I was free to choose, so I chose Ruby. And at the time, Ruby didn't have any tooling or not very much when it came to web applications. So I had to build it all myself.
text: 我们当时在做 Basecamp，而我可以自由选择，所以我选了 Ruby。可那时候 Ruby 在 Web 应用开发这块几乎没什么工具，或者说工具非常少。所以我只能自己把这一整套都做出来。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And that turned into Ruby on Rails, which is still going strong. I'm still very heavily involved with that.
text: 后来这就变成了 Ruby on Rails，而且它现在依然发展得很好。我到现在也还非常深度地参与其中。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I think in some ways, Ruby on Rails is having a little bit of a renaissance now that it is one of the most token efficient ways of building web apps. It's ideally suited for the agent workflows we're dealing with now. We'll see how long that lasts.
text: 我觉得从某种意义上说，Ruby on Rails 现在有点迎来复兴了，因为它是构建 Web 应用时 token 效率最高的方式之一。它跟我们现在在处理的 agent 工作流特别契合。至于这种情况能持续多久，我们再看吧。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Maybe all the agents are gonna be writing machine code or assembler in about five minutes.
text: 没准再过五分钟，所有 agent 就都开始写机器码或者汇编了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So maybe that comes to an end, but for the moment, token efficiency still matters, and it still matters whether the agents produce code that humans are able to read and verify.
text: 所以也许那终究会走到尽头，但至少现在，token 效率依然很重要，而且 agent 产出的代码人类是否能读懂、能验证，也依然很重要。

speaker: Guest
text_en: That may also come to an end at some point, but as it is right now, it's been a fun ride to just see these kinds of projects where I'm scratching my own itch resonate with a much larger community of people who then show up and want to help.
text: 这件事以后某个时候也可能会结束，但就目前来说，看到这类原本只是为了解决我自己需求的项目，能引起更大社区的共鸣，然后大家主动出现、想要帮忙，这一路真的很有意思。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I mean, for Umachi, which has only been around for, what is that, just over six months now, we have what, 400 contributors who've made code changes to the distribution.
text: 我是说，Umachi 到现在也才刚刚存在了六个多月吧，我们现在已经有差不多 400 位贡献者给这个发行版提交过代码改动了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And on top of that, we have tens of thousands of people who've installed it and use it as their daily driver. So I always love that discovery of something new, novel, and inspiring like Ruby.
text: 除此之外，还有几万个人安装了它，并把它当作自己的日常主力系统来用。所以我一直很喜欢那种发现新东西的感觉，那种新鲜、独特、又让人受启发的东西，就像 Ruby 一样。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Or, it sounds weird to talk about discovery of an operating system that's been around since, what, ‘91, but for a lot of people, Linux now is that discovery because they have not been using it on their personal computer, so they're seeing it for the first time.
text: 或者说，把一个从 91 年就存在的操作系统说成是“发现”，听起来有点怪，但对很多人来说，Linux 现在确实就是这样一种发现，因为他们以前并没有在自己的个人电脑上用过它，所以现在等于是第一次见到。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And for me to help a new cohort of Linux users and hopefully even enthusiasts come to be because I'm flattening the curve a little bit, I'm making it easier to get started, I'm making the default installation just look amazing so that they don't feel like they have to invest a hundred hours into tweaking the system to get going, it's really fun.
text: 而对我来说，如果因为我把门槛稍微拉平了一点、让上手变得更容易一点、让默认安装看起来就很惊艳，让他们不用觉得非得先花上一百个小时折腾系统才能开始用，从而帮助新一批 Linux 用户，甚至希望能培养出一些爱好者，这真的很有意思。

speaker: Guest
text_en: But what's also fun, of course, is that both of these things, both Ruby on Rails and Omachi, were not just hobby projects. I love hobby projects, and I will always do those. But I also like to apply them to business.
text: 但当然，另一件同样有意思的事是，这两样东西——Ruby on Rails 和 Omachi——都不只是业余项目。我很喜欢业余项目，而且我也一直都会做。但我也喜欢把它们应用到商业里。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So at 37signals, we built an entire business for 20 plus years on top of Ruby on Rails. We're now running Linux on the majority of developer machines because we now have our own distro. So it's Omachi. I mean, people can choose, right? Can they?
text: 所以在 37signals，我们基于 Ruby on Rails 搭建起了一个持续了 20 多年的完整业务。现在我们大多数开发者机器上跑的也是 Linux，因为我们已经有了自己的发行版，也就是 Omachi。我的意思是，大家当然可以自己选，对吧？他们可以吧？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Well, sort of, kind of. We started with a... with a no-menu choice. And then at some point, it just doesn't make sense anymore. In the same way it would not make sense for someone to be at 37signals and say, I want to write this thing in Django.
text: 嗯，算是吧，有点那个意思。我们一开始是……是不设菜单、自由选择的。后来到了某个阶段，这就不再合理了。就像在 37signals，不会有人说，我想用 Django 来写这个东西。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We're gonna use Python and this other framework, even if you have Ruby on Rails and you're doing that. So we pivoted from an early invitation to play around.
text: 我们要用 Python 和另一个框架，哪怕你们本来就在用 Ruby on Rails 做这些。所以我们从一开始邀请大家随便试试，转向了更明确的方向。

speaker: Guest
text_en: That was what, when I first switched to Linux, I said like, hey, if you want to check it out, check it out.
text: 当初我刚切到 Linux 的时候，我就是会说，嘿，你想试试看就去试试看。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Then when things got a little more serious with Omachi, I just said, let's go all in for everyone who's on the technical side of things, not the iOS developers, of course, but anyone who's working with the web, who's working with Ruby, who's doing DevOps.
text: 后来 Omachi 这边开始更认真推进之后，我就直接说，技术这边的所有人都全面切过去吧。当然 iOS 开发不算，但凡是做 web 的、写 Ruby 的、做 DevOps 的，都应该切到 Linux。

speaker: Guest
text_en: They should be on Linux, because first of all, that's closer to what we deploy. We've always deployed on Linux. We've been a Linux shop on the server side since day one.
text: 他们就该用 Linux，因为首先，那样更接近我们的部署环境。我们一直都是部署在 Linux 上。从第一天起，我们服务端这边就是个 Linux shop。

speaker: Guest
text_en: For developers and system operators, I actually think it is a material advantage to be closer to your production environment and just be more familiar with the tools.
text: 对于开发者和系统运维人员来说，我确实觉得，更贴近你的生产环境、也更熟悉这些工具，是个很实在的优势。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Then on top of that, of course, we are building this distribution and we should have as many hands help out as possible. And given the fact that I'm the CTO of this company, I get to set the technical direction, and this is the direction we're going.
text: 再加上，当然，我们自己也在做这个发行版，理应让尽可能多的人一起帮忙。而且考虑到我是这家公司的 CTO，技术方向由我来定，而这就是我们要走的方向。

speaker: Host
text_en: Can you just, like, do a, like, just a very short recap of, you know, like, how you... And right now, where are you? Like, where, where is the business as a whole?
text: 你能不能简单、很快地回顾一下，比如说，你们是怎么一路走到现在的？还有，你们现在处在什么阶段？就是整个业务目前是什么情况？

speaker: Guest
text_en: And, you know, you keep, you keep building, you keep launching new and exciting and just cool stuff. I think Fizzy was the latest one. Yes. So 37signals was founded in 1999. It started as a web design firm.
text: 而且，你知道，你们一直在做，一直在发布新的、让人兴奋的、很酷的东西。我觉得 Fizzy 是最新的那个。对。37signals 成立于 1999 年，一开始是一家网页设计公司。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And then I joined up in 2001, two years after. And for a couple of years, collaborated with Jason on these consulting projects. And then it was in 2003, we started work on Basecamp, released it in 2004.
text: 然后我在 2001 年加入，也就是两年后。接下来的几年里，我和 Jason 一起做这些咨询项目。后来到了 2003 年，我们开始做 Basecamp，并在 2004 年发布。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Actually, either the day after or the day before Facebook went live, which is kind of a funny coincidence that we were of that same time and cohort.
text: 实际上，Basecamp 上线的时间不是在 Facebook 上线前一天，就是后一天。这个巧合还挺有意思的，说明我们算是同一时代、同一批起来的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And within about a year, we realized this thing was taking off and we went full-time and switched from being a consultancy to being a software company. Awesome. And that's now 22 years ago, a little more than that.
text: 大概一年之内，我们就意识到这东西开始起飞了，于是我们全职投入，也从一家咨询公司转型成了一家软件公司。太棒了。而那已经是 22 年前的事了，甚至还要更久一点。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And in that time, we've released a ton of products.
text: 在这段时间里，我们发布了非常多的产品。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Basecamp was the first, remains the biggest and most important, which is also kind of funny because you sometimes perhaps have this delusion that as you learn more and as you get more experience, you'll get smarter and you'll have better ideas.
text: Basecamp 是第一个产品，到现在也依然是最大、最重要的那个。这其实也挺有意思的，因为有时候你可能会有一种错觉，觉得自己学得越多、经验越丰富，就会变得更聪明，也会有更好的点子。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And like, no, there's tons of people for whom their first idea was the best idea. And I have no shame in saying that Basecamp was the best idea, objectively, in terms of a business that we've ever had.
text: 但其实并不是这样，很多人最初的那个点子，反而就是最好的点子。我一点也不觉得不好意思地说，Basecamp 从商业角度看，客观来说就是我们做过的最好的点子。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And I'm incredibly proud that we've been able to keep that going and growing and flourishing for over 20 years. Very few software companies, let alone software products, can boast of that longevity and legacy.
text: 而且我特别自豪的是，二十多年来，我们一直能让它持续发展、不断成长、保持兴旺。能拥有这么长的寿命和影响力的软件公司，别说软件产品了，真的少之又少。

speaker: Guest
text_en: But we've tried a ton of things over those years and had some other great successes. com, our email service, back in 2020, which was a crazy mission when you think about it.
text: 但这些年里我们试过很多东西，也取得过一些很棒的成功。比如 2020 年推出的 .com，我们的邮件服务，现在回头想想，那真是个疯狂的任务。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Here is a sector completely dominated by a single player, Google, with Gmail. That's a good product. It hasn't really changed since 17 years, but it was really solid and lots of people are perfectly content with it, they think.
text: 这是一个几乎被单一玩家 Google 通过 Gmail 完全统治的领域。那确实是个好产品。17 年来基本没怎么变过，但它一直很扎实，很多人也对它相当满意，至少他们自己是这么觉得的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: They hold this duality in their head where at once they both hate email, but somehow don't connect it to the fact that they're using Gmail, which I find curious.
text: 他们脑子里同时存在一种矛盾：一方面他们讨厌电子邮件，另一方面却又 somehow 没把这件事和自己用的就是 Gmail 联系起来，我觉得这挺有意思的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: But either way, we launched this that is not only a competitor to this very entrenched product that has probably a greater grasp on market share in any major category than any other product I can come to mind of.
text: 但不管怎样，我们推出了这个产品，它不仅是在和一个根基极深的产品竞争，而且这个产品在市场份额上的掌控力，可能比我能想到的任何一个主要品类里的其他产品都还要强。

speaker: Guest
text_en: , I think Gmail is something like 85% of all email traffic, which sounds insane. Maybe it's 80%. It's incredibly high.
text: 我觉得 Gmail 大概占了所有邮件流量的 85%，听起来很夸张。也可能是 80%。总之高得惊人。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Tweet, and then suddenly there's just beautiful flowers. Suddenly email is not a chore. Suddenly you wanna go smell the roses. Suddenly the majority of things that end up in my email are things I wanna read. It's from people I wanna hear from.
text: 发完一条 Tweet，突然之间眼前就是漂亮的花。突然邮件不再是件苦差事了。突然你会想去闻闻玫瑰。突然最后进到我邮箱里的，大多数都是我真想看的东西，都是我想收到消息的人发来的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And that was really the fundamental mission for us with Hey. Can we make email lovable again?
text: 而这对我们做 Hey 来说，真的是最根本的使命。我们能不能让电子邮件重新变得让人喜欢？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Email is so hated by so many people because the systems are so poor, because they're based on the original premise that email is just what universities use for scientists to talk to each other, and scientists have really good manners and will not pester you 52 times about some stupid app they wanna sell you.
text: 之所以那么多人讨厌电子邮件，是因为这些系统做得太差了，因为它们建立在最初那个前提上：电子邮件只是大学里科学家彼此交流用的东西，而科学家都很有礼貌，不会为了卖你某个蠢 app，追着你骚扰 52 次。

speaker: Guest
text_en: No, they're respectful and beautiful, right? Beautiful idea, beautiful thought, beautiful protocol design for those norms and those people.
text: 不是，它们是很尊重人的，也很美，对吧？理念很美，想法很美，为那些规范和那些人设计的协议也很美。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Then you let it into the world at large, and you realize, ah, not everyone is endowed with such norms and such politeness, and especially when salespeople get involved. So you need better defenses.
text: 然后你把它放到更广阔的现实世界里，就会发现，啊，并不是每个人都具备这样的规范和礼貌，尤其是销售一介入之后。所以你就需要更好的防御。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And for me and for us, and for all our many customers, Hey is that defense. It is a way to love email again. And I find that it's really important, actually, to have a grand why. This is all the way back to Viktor Frankl, The Meaning of Man.
text: 对我、对我们，还有对我们的很多客户来说，Hey 就是那道防线。它是一种让人重新爱上电子邮件的方式。而我觉得，真的，拥有一个宏大的“为什么”非常重要。这可以一路追溯到 Viktor Frankl 的《Man's Search for Meaning》。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Finding a why allows you to walk through the snow when it's cold and uncomfortable and annoying, which many things are when you're building with computers. They are cold and uncomfortable and annoying.
text: 找到一个“为什么”，能让你在寒冷、不舒服、又烦人的时候，依然顶着风雪往前走。用电脑做产品时，很多事情就是这样：冰冷、难受、还让人烦。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Now, it shouldn't be that most of the time, but occasionally that will be there. And if you have a really strong why, why are we building this? Who is it for? What are we trying to do to improve the world?
text: 当然，大多数时候不该是这样，但偶尔确实会遇到。如果你有一个非常强的“为什么”——我们为什么要做这个？它是为谁做的？我们想怎样让世界变得更好？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Even if that's not more grand than just letting people love email, it's a lot easier. And it's a lot more enjoyable to then carry whatever burdens you gotta pack if you can set it up that way.
text: 哪怕这个目标没那么宏大，只是让人们重新爱上电子邮件，也会轻松很多。这样一来，你要扛的那些负担也会更容易承受，过程也会更愉快。

speaker: Host
text_en: This is a good time to talk about our season sponsor, WorkOS. Having a strong why is what gets you to building something great.
text: 现在正好可以聊聊我们这一季的赞助商 WorkOS。一个强有力的“为什么”，正是推动你做出伟大产品的动力。

speaker: Host
text_en: But after you build it and start selling it to enterprise customers, they expect things like SAML, SSO, directory sync, audit logs, and fine-grained permissions. And those are not small features, they're systems.
text: 但当你把产品做出来，并开始卖给企业客户之后，他们会期待你提供 SAML、SSO、目录同步、审计日志，以及细粒度权限。这些可不是什么小功能，而是一整套系统。

speaker: Host
text_en: Systems that can take months to build and maintain. WorkOS gives you APIs that enterprise-ready auth and user management in days instead of months, all designed to fit cleanly into your product.
text: 有些系统的开发和维护可能要花上好几个月。WorkOS 提供的 API，能让你在几天内而不是几个月内就做好企业级认证和用户管理，而且都是为了能干净利落地融入你的产品而设计的。

speaker: Host
text_en: That's why companies like OpenAI and Tropic and Cursor run on WorkOS. Focus on building your product. Let WorkOS handle the enterprise infrastructure. With this, let's get back to David and the old way of thinking versus the new way of thinking.
text: 这就是为什么像 OpenAI、Tropic 和 Cursor 这样的公司都在用 WorkOS。专注去打造你的产品吧，把企业级基础设施交给 WorkOS。说到这儿，我们回到 David，以及旧的思维方式和新的思维方式这个话题。

speaker: Host
text_en: Putting your developer hat on, can you talk me through on how you built it? You said it was two years, but was it just one or two people starting to build it? I'm sure as tech, you obviously must have used Ruby on Rails a lot.
text: 从开发者的角度来说，你能跟我讲讲你们是怎么把它做出来的吗？你刚才说花了两年，但一开始是不是就只有一两个人在做？我猜在技术上，你们肯定大量用了 Ruby on Rails。

speaker: Host
text_en: And then probably some native stuff as well. But the two years seems a lot, especially because you're a small company. You're nimble. You're a great developer. You hire great developers. Suddenly it's been two years. What took so long for...
text: 然后可能也用了些原生开发的东西。但两年还是感觉很久，尤其你们还是一家小公司。你们动作很快，你是很强的开发者，你招的也是很强的开发者。结果一转眼就两年了。到底是什么花了这么久……

speaker: Host
text_en: And of course, it's a beautiful product, but right on the surface, I think as developers, we might have this thing where I look at it as like, two years with a talented team. That's the Hacker News quip to basically everything, right?
text: 当然了，这产品确实很漂亮，但只看表面的话，我觉得作为开发者，我们可能都会有那种想法：一个有才华的团队做了两年？这不就是 Hacker News 对几乎所有东西都会冒出来的那种吐槽吗？

speaker: Host
text_en: Like, well, I could have built that in a weekend. I mean, famously stated with Dropbox that I could have built that in a weekend. We could have at the original iPod when it launched.
text: 就像会说，哦，这东西我一个周末就能做出来。Dropbox 当年就很经典，很多人都说这玩意儿我一个周末就能做出来。最初的 iPod 发布时也是一样。

speaker: Host
text_en: It was like, five gigabytes, no Wi-Fi, whatever, less speed than Nomad Lane. So I get that because I also have that same instinct. I think that is our hubris as developers. We think, we are gods and we can make anything happen in no time at all.
text: 当时不就是五个 GB，没有 Wi‑Fi，诸如此类的，速度还没 Nomad Lane 快。所以我能理解这种想法，因为我自己也有同样的本能反应。我觉得这就是我们开发者的傲慢：我们觉得自己像神一样，什么都能在极短时间内搞定。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And you totally could. You can make a prototype happen in these days faster than in a weekend, right? Like in a few hours, we should be able to have... Just kick off an agent, yeah. But figuring out what you actually wanna build takes a lot longer.
text: 而且你完全可以。现在要做个原型，速度比一个周末还快，对吧？比如几小时内，我们就应该能做出个……直接启动一个 agent 就行了，对。但真正花时间的是，搞清楚你到底想做什么。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And arriving at something that's worth publishing takes longer still. At least it does for us. And I think it does for anyone who arrives at anything good. And the original Hey construction was just me on the technical side.
text: 而且，要做到值得发布的程度，还要花更久。至少我们是这样。我觉得任何真正做出好东西的人，基本也都这样。最初 Hey 的开发阶段，技术这边其实就只有我一个人。

speaker: Guest
text_en: This is actually how we've started the majority of our major products.
text: 其实我们大多数重要产品，一开始都是这么做的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Either it's just me, sometimes it's one additional developer, but it's a tiny, tiny team until we have a shape, until we have an architecture and we have a direction of where the product is going to go.
text: 要么就只有我，有时候再加一个开发，但在我们把产品的大致形态、架构，还有未来要往哪走这些事情想清楚之前，团队都会非常非常小。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I've found that you actually go slower if you pour a bunch of people into a direction that is uncertain. If you don't know what you want, a million people is not gonna build it for you. You have to figure out what you want.
text: 我发现，如果你把一大堆人投入到一个还不确定的方向里，反而会更慢。你要是连自己想要什么都不知道，再来一百万人也帮你造不出来。你得先搞清楚自己到底想要什么。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We can talk about this later, but this is where AI's very recent progress is changing things dramatically. It is now quicker to arrive at what do I want. But for Hey, it was me.
text: 这个我们之后可以再聊，但 AI 最近的进展，正在非常剧烈地改变这件事。现在更快就能搞清楚“我到底想要什么”。但做 Hey 的时候，还是我一个人。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And then it was Jason and one designer, two designers, very, very small team trying to figure out the shape, trying to figure out if you're taking on Gmail, you can't just do Gmail in blue. No one's going to buy that.
text: 后来是 Jason，再加一个设计师、两个设计师，就这么一个非常非常小的团队，去摸索产品的形态。因为如果你要挑战 Gmail，你不能只是做一个蓝色版的 Gmail。没人会买账的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: No one's going to be interested in that. It's got to be. Novel, which means it, well, not just novel, it's got to be good.
text: 也不会有人对那种东西感兴趣。它必须得有新意，不过也不只是有新意，它还得真的好用。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It's got to solve problems that people haven't even articulated they have with Gmail because the articulation people have of their problems with Gmail is, I hate email, which as we talked about, is a bit of a misdirection.
text: 它得解决一些问题，而且是那些用户甚至都还没明确意识到自己在 Gmail 上有这些问题。因为大家对 Gmail 的问题，通常只会说一句“我讨厌电子邮件”，但就像我们刚才聊的，这其实有点把方向带偏了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: My contention is, you hate Gmail and not just Gmail, but most email systems built on the old way of anyone has access to your inbox and all that stuff.
text: 我的观点是，你讨厌的是 Gmail，而且不只是 Gmail，而是大多数那种老一套的邮件系统——谁都能碰到你的收件箱之类的那种东西。

speaker: Guest
text_en: But figuring that out, figuring the shape out, takes a while, and it's also fun to do in this way where you noodle with it, and you don't have infinite capacity. The original basecamp was built the same way. It was just me on the technical side.
text: 但要把这个想明白，把整体形状摸清楚，是要花点时间的。而且用这种方式做也挺有意思的，你会反复琢磨它，同时你的产能也不是无限的。最初的 Basecamp 也是这么做出来的。当时技术这边就只有我一个人。

speaker: Host
text_en: Is this a shape-up methodology? There's shape-up thinking in trying to actually endow the designer with an intention of how should it work, not just how should it look, and figuring out, it's also how it should look.
text: 这是 shape-up 方法论吗？这里面有一种 shape-up 的思路，就是要真正赋予设计师一种意图：不只是它该长什么样，还包括它该怎么运作；当然，也要弄清楚它该长什么样。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Products should be beautiful, and they should be unique and appealing and so forth, so that also takes time. But figuring out how it should work is primary.
text: 产品应该是美的，也应该是独特的、有吸引力的，诸如此类，所以这也需要时间。但首先还是要弄清楚它该怎么运作。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Figuring out where's the epicenter, what's the most important part, and teasing all that apart.
text: 要找出它的核心点在哪里，最重要的部分是什么，再把这些东西一点点理清。

speaker: Guest
text_en: But with Hey, as with all the major products we've done, we start with an absolutely tiny team, often just one individual on the programming side and then one or two individuals on the design side. And then we go, we go, we go, we go.
text: 但做 Hey 的时候，和我们做其他所有重要产品一样，一开始都是一个极小的团队，通常程序这边就一个人，设计这边再加一两个人。然后我们就一路往前推，推，推，推。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Suddenly something clicks and we go like, this is good. There's something here. And then there's a bit of a ramp. We take on a few more people. And then when we get within maybe the last 20%, we go, okay, now we know what the terrain looks like.
text: 突然某个时刻就打通了，我们会觉得：这个不错，这里面有东西。然后就会进入一个小幅爬坡阶段，我们再多加几个人。等做到最后大概 20% 的时候，我们就会说，好，现在我们知道这片地形长什么样了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We can go way faster if everyone piles in.
text: 如果大家一起上，我们就能快得多。

speaker: Host
text_en: So one thing that is super interesting, and you might take it for granted, but it's very different to how most startups that raise VC money, which I'm very familiar with, and big companies, Uber, Facebook, you name it, the way projects would start there is you take the product manager who works with maybe maybe half a designer and comes up with a spec and then developers get involved later.
text: 有一件事特别有意思，你们可能已经习以为常了，但这和大多数拿了 VC 投资的创业公司——我对这类公司很熟——以及大公司，比如 Uber、Facebook 之类的做法很不一样。那些地方通常是这样启动项目的：先由产品经理，可能再配上半个设计师，一起写出一份 spec，然后开发人员后面才会介入。

speaker: Host
text_en: And what I'm hearing, what is very novel to me is you take one or two designers and a developer. How do you think about designers even? You recently hired a designer, Zoltan actually, who I'm chatting with on the side, a great guy.
text: 但我现在听到的、对我来说很新鲜的是，你们会先上一个或两个设计师，再加一个开发。你们到底是怎么理解设计师这个角色的？你们最近还招了个设计师，Zoltan，我私下也在和他聊，人特别棒。

speaker: Host
text_en: But my sense is, you think of designers a little bit different than potentially the rest of the industry does. We very much do. Designers at 37signals are not just here to make a spec look pretty. They're here to find what the spec should be.
text: 但我的感觉是，你们对设计师的看法，和行业里大多数公司的理解有点不一样。确实非常不一样。在 37signals，设计师不只是把 spec 做得更好看，他们是来弄清楚 spec 本来应该是什么的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: They're product managers in many ways. They are the finders of the how and the why in many cases, deducing, in some cases, customer feedback, in other cases, just pure intuition, and distilling that into what should we build and how should it work.
text: 很多时候，他们其实就是产品经理。他们往往是在寻找“怎么做”和“为什么这么做”的人：有时是从客户反馈里推导出来，有时纯粹靠直觉，然后把这些提炼成我们到底该做什么、它应该怎么运作。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And then on top of that, they're also responsible for building it. They're responsible for doing the CSS. They're responsible for doing the HTML.
text: 除此之外，他们还要负责把它做出来。CSS 他们负责，HTML 他们也负责。

speaker: Guest
text_en: They're quite often responsible, at least dabbling in the JavaScript and the Ruby code to get to something functional. And now with agent acceleration, they do the whole thing.
text: 很多时候，他们还会参与一点 JavaScript 和 Ruby 代码，至少会动手试一试，把东西做成可用的功能。现在有了 agent acceleration，他们基本上能把整套都做下来。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Not necessarily as it will be merged, but the whole thing in terms of, here's the final shape and design of what it should look like. But I do think we are very peculiar in this sense.
text: 不一定是最终会被 merge 的那版代码，但至少能把完整的东西做出来，也就是：这就是它最终应该呈现的形态和设计。不过我也承认，我们在这点上确实挺特别的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And we have found this when we've been trying to hire designers, that many designers working at other companies are not used to also wearing the product manager hat, figuring out what we should build, and wearing the implementation hat, shaping it into CSS and HTML.
text: 而且我们在招聘设计师时也发现了这一点：很多在别的公司工作的设计师，并不习惯同时戴上产品经理的帽子，去想清楚我们该做什么；也不习惯戴上实现者的帽子，把它落实成 CSS 和 HTML。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I've found that when you combine these three hats into one, you have an individual who know the materials they're working with, know how they stretch, know which way the seam is supposed to be cut, and therefore works natively with the fabric of the internet.
text: 我发现，当你把这三顶帽子合到一个人身上时，这个人就会懂自己在处理的材料，懂它们会怎么延展，懂接缝该朝哪个方向裁，因此也就能很自然地顺着互联网这块“布料”来工作。

speaker: Guest
text_en: When you're working directly in CSS, when you're working directly in HTML, you're just much more in tune with what this medium wants. And I find that that's probably quite similar if you're a jewelry designer. You should know the properties of gold.
text: 当你直接用 CSS、直接用 HTML 工作时，你对这种媒介真正想要什么的感觉就是会强很多。我觉得这大概和珠宝设计师很像。你就应该了解黄金的特性。

speaker: Guest
text_en: You should know how it bends and the strength. An architect should have some engineering understanding of load-bearing structures and so on.
text: 你应该知道它怎么弯折、强度如何。建筑师也应该对承重结构之类的工程原理有一些理解。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Not to the degree that the architect is just going to design the whole thing and then we start pouring concrete. You still have engineers helping you out.
text: 当然也不是说建筑师一个人把整个东西设计完，然后我们就直接开始浇混凝土了。你还是需要工程师来帮你。

speaker: Guest
text_en: But the more you understand the materials you're working with, the more you're likely to come up with something that cuts along the grain and therefore ends up feeling correct, feeling good. Just a quick hop to Apple.
text: 但你越了解自己在使用的材料，就越有可能做出顺着纹理来的东西，最后呈现出来也就会更对味、更舒服。顺便快速跳到 Apple 这个话题。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I think this is one of the reasons why some of the historic super fans like Daring Fireball and others, Gruber, have been disappointed by the new direction, is that Apple used to stand for these exquisitely designed native Mac applications, which is a dying breed.
text: 我觉得这也是为什么像 Daring Fireball、Gruber 这些老牌超级粉丝，会对现在的新方向感到失望。因为 Apple 过去代表的是那种设计极其精妙的原生 Mac 应用，而这种东西现在已经越来越少了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Like, they're essentially dead. Now we have Electron, which we can talk about that too. It gets way too much hate in my book. There's crappy imitations of that, but it's just a web in a box.
text: 可以说基本已经死了。现在我们有了 Electron，这个我们也可以聊。按我的看法，它背的锅实在太多了。当然也有很多很烂的模仿品，但它本质上就是把网页装进一个盒子里。

speaker: Guest
text_en: But the disappointment with losing that sense, and I think it's about the same thing, that the Mac, its native feel, has a stretch to it.
text: 但大家失望的是那种感觉的消失了，而我觉得说的其实还是同一件事：Mac 那种原生的手感，本身是有一种弹性的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Like the button placements, everything you would call a native application, either feels synthetic or it feels authentic. And today, it's all synthetic. There's nothing authentic about it left. And I think, for the web, it's the same thing.
text: 比如按钮摆放这些，你会称之为原生应用的那些东西，要么让人感觉很生硬，要么让人感觉很真实。而今天基本上全都是生硬的，已经没什么真实感可言了。我觉得放到 web 上也是一样。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Now, the web is a much, much larger platform, and therefore, it's gotten much more attention. So there are way more people working on that quality of it.
text: 当然，web 是一个大得多得多的平台，所以它得到的关注也多得多。因此，投入在这方面品质上的人也多得多。

speaker: Guest
text_en: But at the large companies, it's exceptionally rare to non-existent to have that kind of dynamic. I think some of that is going to change. Agent acceleration is going to empower designers to be more capable in these ways.
text: 但在大公司里，这种动态机制极其罕见，几乎等于不存在。我觉得这其中有一部分会改变。Agent acceleration 会让设计师在这些方面变得更有能力。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So the industry is coming a little towards our fundamental stands, which is funny, too, because the same is true on the programming side.
text: 所以整个行业正在一点点朝我们最根本的立场靠拢，这也挺有意思的，因为在编程这边也是一样。

speaker: Guest
text_en: When I talked about Basecamp being a product of just me on the programming side for launch, that for so long sounded unambitious or even wrong, or even to the point of lying from some quarters of the internet, where like, yeah, but you can't build anything real, anything meaningful, anything big unless you have a team.
text: 我之前说过，Basecamp 在发布时，编程这边基本就是我一个人做出来的。很长一段时间里，这听起来都像是不够有野心，甚至是错的，甚至在互联网上有些人会觉得你在撒谎，说什么，对啊，但如果没有一个团队，你根本不可能做出真正的、有意义的、规模够大的东西。

speaker: Guest
text_en: That's much larger because it's just going to be a toy product. And my insight from the start was, that's, of course, bullshit, because you just haven't used Ruby on Rails.
text: 因为那样做出来的只会是个玩具产品。我从一开始的看法就是，这当然是胡扯，因为你只是还没用过 Ruby on Rails。

speaker: Guest
text_en: You just haven't used the acceleration that's possible if you use better tools. Now, we're all realizing that. We're realizing, oh, so if you use agent acceleration, a single individual actually can build something high-value.
text: 你只是还没体验过，用更好的工具时能带来的那种加速。现在我们都开始意识到这一点了。我们开始明白，哦，原来如果你用 agent acceleration，一个人其实也能做出高价值的东西。

speaker: Guest
text_en: That's because you take a team. Yes. And that's just fun to see that the industry is coming towards, oh, smaller teams are better because now the cost savings you have on the logarithmic curve on communication cost starts to be relevant.
text: 那是因为你等于把一个团队的能力都集中起来了。对。而且看到整个行业开始转向这种想法也很有意思：小团队更好，因为当沟通成本那条对数曲线带来的节省开始变得重要时，这一点就会特别明显。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And this is one of the things, maybe we can talk about this, where agent acceleration is really changing the bargain between junior developers and senior developers. Let's talk about this.
text: 这也是其中一个点，也许我们可以聊聊这个：agent acceleration 确实正在改变初级开发者和资深开发者之间的交换关系。我们来聊聊这个。

speaker: Host
text_en: But before we go into that, do I feel that you very much value software engineering as a craft, which is very obvious.
text: 不过在聊这个之前，我感觉你非常看重软件工程这门手艺，这点很明显。

speaker: Host
text_en: But what I'm sensing is you're valuing design, user experience design, designing on software design, like building stuff that feels good, may that be software, hardware. You also value that as a craft, and you look for it. These two things.
text: 但我感受到的是，你也很看重设计、用户体验设计、软件设计，也就是做出那种用起来感觉很好的东西，不管是软件还是硬件。你也把这个当成一门手艺，而且你会主动去寻找它。就是这两点。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Do I sense this correctly? Absolutely. I mean, I think aesthetics is truth. When something is beautiful, it's likely to be correct.
text: 我这么感觉对吗？完全对。我的意思是，我觉得美感就是真理。一个东西如果很美，它大概率就是对的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I think this is true in mathematics, this is true in physics, this is true in a lot of different domains, that when you arrive at something that has the correct aesthetic quality, it's like we have an intuition that guides us towards that level of beauty because it also happens to be correct and noble and something to aspire for.
text: 我觉得这在数学里是这样，在物理里也是这样，在很多不同领域里都成立：当你抵达某种具有正确审美品质的东西时，就像是我们内在有一种直觉，会把我们引向那种层次的美，因为它恰好也是正确的、高贵的、值得追求的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I also happen to believe it's what makes people happy. Being surrounded by beautiful, well-functioning objects is a key part of happiness. In fact, I'll put it in a negative way, too.
text: 我也相信，这正是让人快乐的东西。被美丽、好用、运转良好的物件包围，是幸福感里非常关键的一部分。其实我也可以反过来说。

speaker: Guest
text_en: One of the great sources of anxiety and frustration is when everything is shit.
text: 焦虑和挫败感的一大来源，就是所有东西都烂透了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: When everything is laggy, when that touch interface doesn't register, when you have to restart it, when you're calling a travel agent that can't do something because their old shitty cobalt system won't let them.
text: 当一切都卡得要命，当那个触控界面怎么点都没反应，当你不得不重启它，当你打电话给旅行代理，对方却什么都办不了，因为他们那套又老又烂的 cobalt 系统根本不让他们操作。

speaker: Guest
text_en: The world is full of not just in shitification, that is things that went from being good to being bad, to just plain bad, just plain awful.
text: 这个世界到处都是这种不只是“劣化”的东西——也就是那些从好变坏的东西——而是彻底坏掉，糟得不能再糟。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And I think it is a serious source of malaise for civilization that we could literally raise the bar of human happiness if we were surrounded by more beautiful items, more beautiful systems, both in the sense of its aesthetic exterior qualities, but just as much in terms of its aesthetic interior qualities, because I find those two things are usually in perfect harmony.
text: 我觉得，这其实是文明层面上一种很严重的不适来源。因为如果我们身边有更多美的物件、更多美的系统，人类幸福感的门槛真的会被抬高。不只是它外在审美上的美，也同样包括它内在结构上的美，因为我发现这两者通常是完全和谐一致的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: The reason why Steve Jobs cared about the inside of the box was because he intuitively knew that the kind of people who care about the layout of the printboard will be the kind of people who sweat the details on the user interface, will be the kind of people who sweat the ergonomics of opening the case.
text: Steve Jobs 会在意盒子里面的原因，是因为他凭直觉知道：会在意电路板布局的人，也会是在用户界面细节上精雕细琢的人，也会是在打开机箱时的人体工学上反复打磨的人。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So I think there is essentially no choice if you are a person who is attracted to these aesthetics, which I think is everyone. There's just varying levels of awareness about whether you are or not, but that you want to make it all beautiful.
text: 所以我觉得，如果你是会被这种审美吸引的人——而我认为其实每个人都是，只是有没有意识到程度不同——那你基本上别无选择，你就是会想把一切都做得更美。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And for me, Ruby in particular has been the seminal language because it produces the most beautiful code. In my book, there's barely even competition. Like there are other things that can be beautiful in a way.
text: 对我来说，Ruby 尤其是一门奠基性的语言，因为它能写出最美的代码。在我看来，几乎都谈不上有什么竞争对手。当然，别的东西也可以以某种方式很美。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Like I find, looking at small talk, for example, very beautiful in its minimalism, but not the house I want to live in. Ruby...
text: 比如说，我觉得 Smalltalk 看起来就很美，美在它的极简，但那不是我想住进去的房子。Ruby……

speaker: Guest
text_en: Here's the house I want to live in because it's got that aesthetic quality while not being rigid about its ideology, which is a very rare aspect too.
text: 这才是我想住进去的房子，因为它既有那种审美品质，又不会在自己的理念上过于僵硬，这一点也非常难得。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I more often find, now we can refer to I've again, is that when someone is obsessed in this way, they are a little narrow-minded. Like that's the trade-off. That's the price.
text: 不过我现在更常发现——这里又可以提到 iVe 了——当一个人以这种方式着迷时，往往也会有点狭隘。就像这是种权衡，是你要付出的代价。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And I find that Ruby has somehow managed to be both broad-scoped yet also intensely focused on this. But overall, we have to have beautiful things. We have to work with beautiful tools. We have to produce beautiful, fluid interactions.
text: 而且我发现，Ruby 不知怎么就做到了既覆盖面很广，同时又在这件事上高度聚焦。但总的来说，我们就是得拥有美的东西。我们得使用美的工具。我们得做出优美、流畅的交互。

speaker: Host
text_en: This is how we should see ourselves as craftspeople, that we care about polishing it until there are no splinters left. How is AI changing how you work? And how do you think it's changing your craft?
text: 这就是我们作为手艺人该如何看待自己：我们在乎把东西打磨到连一根毛刺都不剩。AI 正在怎样改变你的工作方式？你觉得它又是怎样改变你的这门手艺的？

speaker: Host
text_en: Or just, let's just talk about the craft of, again, you're hiring people in 37 Signals who similarly care about design and software, crafts quality. How is changing what you get out of the craft or how it's making it better or worse in some ways?
text: 或者就说回这门手艺吧。毕竟你在 37 Signals 招的人，也同样在乎设计、软件和工艺质量。它是怎样改变你从这门手艺里获得的东西的，或者说它在哪些方面让它变得更好或更糟？

speaker: Host
text_en: I just want to, you know, start with like, how has your view changed? Because the last time you talked in length about this, that was on Lex Friedman's podcast. And you were still, rightfully so, very skeptical of AI. It was different set of tools.
text: 我就是想先从这个开始：你的看法是怎么变的？因为你上一次比较系统地谈这个，还是在 Lex Friedman 的播客上。那时候你对 AI 还是非常怀疑的，这也完全合理。那会儿的工具也不一样。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It had been work as well. And I think you went there bashing it pretty hard, but things have changed since. This is a nuanced point, and maybe it's self-serving, but I don't actually think my opinions have changed.
text: 而且那也已经成了工作的一部分。我觉得你当时去那期节目时，对它批得挺狠的，不过这之后情况确实变了。这一点其实很微妙，也许听起来像是在给自己找补，但我并不觉得我的观点真的变了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: What has changed is the circumstances and the facts. Which is something I called out on that Joe one and in many other writings was, right from the get-go, I could see that we had something new and novel here that was going to change things.
text: 变的是环境和事实。这也是我在 Joe 那期里，还有很多其他文章里提到过的：从一开始我就看得出来，这里出现了某种新的、前所未有的东西，它会改变很多事情。

speaker: Guest
text_en: ChatGPT, it's launched, what, three years ago, was clearly and obviously, even at the time, something you would mark on a timeline. You're like, here are all the important things that happened in the history of computer science or the world.
text: ChatGPT 是三年前上线的吧，很明显，哪怕在当时，它也是那种你会在时间线上专门标出来的事件。你会说，这里是计算机科学史上，或者世界历史上，所有重要事件发生的节点。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Yoinks, there is the launch of ChatGPT.
text: 哎呀，ChatGPT 的发布就在这儿。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And interacting with computers in this way and seeing them reason, even if that's still a disputed term, perhaps, but to me, it seemed obvious that these things were freaking smart, smarter than me in many ways.
text: 用这种方式和计算机交互，看着它们进行推理——虽然这词可能还是有争议——但对我来说，很明显，这些东西聪明得要命，很多方面都比我聪明。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Whether those smarts came from parenting weights and data, somewhat. We don't know how human consciousness works. We don't know how human wisdom or intelligence works barely.
text: 至于这种聪明是不是来自参数权重和数据，某种程度上是吧。我们并不知道人类意识是怎么运作的。我们甚至也几乎不知道人类的智慧或智能是怎么运作的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So let's not be so categorical about what constitutes consciousness or intelligence. At least I find no utility in that distinction, even if it's fun to ponder.
text: 所以，别那么武断地去定义什么才算意识，什么才算智能。至少对我来说，这种区分没什么实际意义，哪怕想想挺有意思。

speaker: Guest
text_en: But what I found with the early models and the early ergonomics where it was autocomplete, where it was co-pilot and cursor in your editor trying to guess the next character. It would be sometimes littering it, right? Yes. I found it infuriating.
text: 但我在早期模型和早期交互方式上发现的问题是，那时候基本就是自动补全，就是你编辑器里的 co-pilot 和 cursor 在猜你下一个字符要打什么。它有时候会把整个过程搞得一团糟，对吧？对。我觉得特别烦。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I found it as, we're trying to have a conversation. You won't let me finish the sentence. You're constantly trying to, was this what you meant? Was this what you meant? You're like, shut the hell up. Can I just finish a thought?
text: 我的感觉是，我们明明是在对话。可你就是不让我把一句话说完。你老是在那儿打断我：你是这个意思吗？你是这个意思吗？真让人想说，闭嘴行不行？能不能让我先把一个想法说完？

speaker: Guest
text_en: And I thought, even if it is capable of occasionally accelerating, it's also wrong so often that that acceleration feels like a nuisance, even if it's somehow net positive, which it wasn't for me.
text: 我当时想，就算它偶尔确实能提速，但它出错的频率也太高了，以至于那种提速更像是一种打扰。哪怕总体上也许是正收益——反正对我来说并不是。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Or maybe I gave up too soon, but I just did not enjoy that. I didn't think the models were good enough. I thought the way of using the models with autocomplete versus agent harnesses was just dreadful and annoying.
text: 也可能是我放弃得太早了，但我就是不喜欢那种体验。我觉得那些模型还不够好。我也觉得，相比 agent harnesses，用自动补全的方式来使用模型，真的又糟糕又烦人。

speaker: Guest
text_en: In fact, to the point that I got a little pessimistic about the direction of the industry for a hot second because I thought this was what we were all going to do. We're all going to sit and do tap, tap, tap. No, thank you.
text: 事实上，甚至有那么一小会儿，我对整个行业的发展方向都有点悲观了，因为我以为这就是我们以后都要做的事：大家都坐在那里 tap tap tap。不了，谢谢。

speaker: Host
text_en: Well, Cursor even have, I even got one of these. One of their swags was a tap key. Exactly. Which felt very, and I, I haven't, like, I got it from them. It's really cool, very well designed and all that beautiful design. But dystopian. Dystopian.
text: 嗯，Cursor 甚至还有，我自己都拿到过一个。他们有个周边是个 Tab 键。没错。那感觉特别，而且我还没怎么，就是我从他们那儿拿到的。东西确实很酷，设计也很好看，各方面都很漂亮。但很反乌托邦。反乌托邦。

speaker: Guest
text_en: When I see that and I remember that was a meme for a while, just we only need three characters on the keyboard, right?
text: 我一看到那个，就想起那阵子有个梗：我们键盘上其实只需要三个字符，对吧？

speaker: Guest
text_en: I thought of that episode of The Simpsons where Homer puts a mechanical bird on the keyboard that just dips down and hits enter because all he's been doing is hit enter, except suddenly there's a warning about the nuclear core overloading and the bird just hits enter and the whole thing burns down.
text: 我就想到《The Simpsons》里那一集，Homer 在键盘上放了只机械鸟，它会低头去按 enter，因为他平时干的事就只是按 enter。结果突然跳出一个提示，说核反应堆核心要过载了，那只鸟还是照样按了 enter，最后整个地方都烧了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I'm like, wow, that's quite a parallel. The Simpsons really does predict everything. But I did not like that style of using it as much as I retained my enthusiasm for the general direction of travel, because it truly is amazing.
text: 我当时就想，哇，这个类比也太贴了。《The Simpsons》还真是什么都能预言。不过我并没有那么喜欢那种具体的使用方式，虽然我对整体发展方向的热情还在，因为这东西确实很惊人。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And the amazement to me, I tried to embrace as a tutor model, as a pair programmer who doesn't drive. It was amazing to have ChatGPT and the other models just be there for like, I don't understand this fully. Here's a piece of code.
text: 而对我来说，这种惊艳感，我是尽量用一种导师模型来接受它的：把它当成一个不掌舵的结对程序员。有 ChatGPT 和其他模型在那儿，真的很神奇。比如我会说，我没完全理解这个。这儿有一段代码。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Here's a question. Can you tell me why it works like that? Can you tell me what's wrong with it? Because that's how I've been using the internet since day one, right? That's what Google was for me. Here's an error message. Here's a concept.
text: 这儿有个问题。你能告诉我它为什么会这样工作吗？你能告诉我它哪里有问题吗？因为我从第一天开始就是这么用互联网的，对吧？Google 对我来说就是干这个的。给你一条报错信息。给你一个概念。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Maybe I find something on Stack Overflow with some passive-aggressive nerd telling everyone why he's so smart and then at the bottom, there's the solution I'm looking for. Or I don't find it at all, and that's just kind of frustrating.
text: 也许我能在 Stack Overflow 上找到点东西，上面有个带点被动攻击味道的 nerd 在那儿告诉所有人自己有多聪明，然后在最底下，才有我想要的答案。或者我根本找不到，那就挺让人沮丧的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: With the ChatGPT model, I very often got a really good explanation. Yeah, this was actually, I talked with a game developer, Jonas Tyroller, who built this really cool best-selling game. I loved playing it.
text: 但用 ChatGPT 这类模型时，我经常能得到非常好的解释。对，这个其实也是，我之前和一位游戏开发者 Jonas Tyroller 聊过，他做了一款特别酷、而且卖得非常好的游戏。我特别喜欢玩。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And this was during this time of the top completion. And he said that in his, the way he works is he just turned off all auto-completions in his ID because he got annoyed by it.
text: 这还是在那阵子自动补全特别火的时候。他说按他的工作方式，他直接把 IDE 里的所有自动补全都关了，因为他被那玩意儿搞烦了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And then every now and then he went to ChatGPT to ask something or have a longer thing. And then he had the mode of like, I'm thinking and I'm doing this stuff. Oh, I need some help. Okay, here's the specifics. And I'm taking it.
text: 然后他只是偶尔去 ChatGPT 问点东西，或者让它处理一段更长的内容。这样他就处在一种模式里：我在思考，我在做这些事。哦，我需要点帮助。好，那我把具体情况给你，然后我再接着往下做。

speaker: Host
text_en: And somehow it felt that, you know, like he just, he was in the zone the whole day by controlling it. And somehow those habits, it sounds like, you know, you're saying the same thing. It kind of took it away from you. Exactly. Exactly.
text: 而且不知怎么的，这样感觉就像，你懂的，他靠自己控制节奏，一整天都能保持心流状态。听起来那些习惯，你说的其实也是同一回事。它有点把这种状态从你身上拿走了。没错，没错。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And I did get a little worried that that was going to be the direction that we're all going to be the bird and I didn't want to be the bird. Then I was like, well, what should I do instead? Maybe like farming potatoes.
text: 我当时确实有点担心，这会不会就是我们所有人接下来要走的方向——都变成那只鸟，而我不想当那只鸟。后来我就想，那我该改做什么呢？要不去种土豆吧。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Like that's a long tradition here in Denmark. Maybe I could take that up. But then thankfully, two things happened. A, Claude code in, what is that? Starts in the spring, gets going sort of over the summer.
text: 毕竟这在 Denmark 也是个由来已久的传统。也许我可以干这个。不过还好，后来发生了两件事。第一，Claude Code 在——那叫什么来着——春天开始出现，到夏天慢慢发展起来。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Then by the fall has some traction on a new way of using agents to help you code with the agent harnesses. Right. This is really where we transition from AI to agents. Suddenly the AI has tools. It can use Bash.
text: 然后到了秋天，一种用 agent harness 来辅助你写代码的新方式开始有了些起色。对吧，这其实就是我们从 AI 过渡到 agents 的节点。AI 一下子有了工具，它可以用 Bash。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It can use everything you got on your terminal. It can call the internet in for appropriate information. It just is capable of doing more than just reasoning about a thing you gave it or input from a source context file. And then the models.
text: 它可以调用你终端里的一切。它还能上网获取合适的信息。它不再只是对你给它的某个东西，或者某个上下文文件里的输入做推理了，它是真的能做更多事了。然后还有模型本身。

speaker: Guest
text_en: 5, to me, is the other one of the other points we're going to have on the line where it's the first model that continuously and consistently would shock me with the quality of its output, the quality of its analysis on the basis of vague inputs, and even more importantly, the quality of its output.
text: 对我来说，5 也是我们这条发展线上另一个关键节点。这是第一个会持续、稳定地让我被输出质量震惊的模型——不管是它基于模糊输入做分析的质量，还是更重要的，它最终产出的质量。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It produced code I wanted to merge without very much, if any, alteration. And if I did want to do alteration, I could tell it and it would remember and it would not make the same mistake next time.
text: 它生成的代码我几乎不用改，甚至完全不用改，就想直接合并进去。就算我确实想改，我也可以告诉它，它会记住，下次不会再犯同样的错。

speaker: Host
text_en: That, to me, the combination of those two things was the unlock. And you have a high bar. Like, you have a really high bar. I have an incredibly high bar.
text: 对我来说，这两点结合在一起，才是真正的突破口。而且你的标准很高。是真的非常高。我自己的标准也高得离谱。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I, as we've talked about now at length, like the aesthetics of the output really matters if I'm going to look at it and I'm going to review it. I'm going to give you another anecdote in a second where those things don't even play in.
text: 就像我们刚才已经详细聊过的，如果我要看它的输出、要审查它，那输出在美感上的表现真的很重要。等下我再给你讲个例子，在那个场景里这些因素甚至都不重要。

speaker: Guest
text_en: But when I'm using agents to work on Ruby code, I want their code to look as good as mine. I'm not going to merge their stuff if it's sloppy.
text: 但当我用 agents 来处理 Ruby 代码时，我希望它们写出来的代码看起来跟我的一样好。要是写得很潦草，我是不会合并的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: No more than I would merge the work of a junior developer who has not yet fully internalized our style and so forth. So I want it to be on par and on parity. And the early models just couldn't. That didn't mean they couldn't produce working software.
text: 这跟我不会合并一个还没完全内化我们代码风格之类规范的初级开发者的代码，是一个道理。所以我希望它能达到同样的水准、真正对齐。早期的模型就是做不到。但这也不代表它们写不出能跑的软件。

speaker: Guest
text_en: At least some of the time, they could. And very impressive. I mean, I remember when I did my first snake game and I'm like, holy smokes, I've been wanting to do this since I was six years old. Like I've been wanting to, I have this idea.
text: 至少有些时候，它们是能做到的。而且非常惊艳。我记得我第一次让它做 snake game 的时候，我心想，天哪，这可是我从六岁起就一直想做的事。就像我一直都想把这个点子做出来。

speaker: Host
text_en: I want to get it into a game. And I was able to see that in, I don't know, a few 30 seconds. It was done with the game out of copy-paste, the HTML. Magical experience, right?
text: 我想把它做成一个游戏。结果我居然在，怎么说呢，几十秒里就看到了。复制粘贴一下 HTML，游戏就出来了。那种体验很神奇，对吧？

speaker: Guest
text_en: So I think that ramp was very interesting because it actually took a while until we found the form factor of the agent harness, of the terminal interface that to me was the big unlock from this is interesting.
text: 所以我觉得那段爬坡过程特别有意思，因为我们其实花了挺久，才找到 agent harness、终端界面这种产品形态。对我来说，那才是让它从“这挺有意思”变成真正突破口的关键。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I want to have a conversation with it to, I want it to write my code. I will now start any project I'm starting with. I'm starting Agent first. And that's a massive shift. 5 dropped. Now, there are other people who have different points.
text: 我想跟它对话，我想让它帮我写代码。现在我开始任何项目，都会先从 Agent 入手。这是个巨大的转变。5 一出来就变了。当然，也有人有不同看法。

speaker: Guest
text_en: 0 or maybe some GPT-5 too. There are other earlier checkpoints, but there, I do feel like there's a general consensus I can lean up against that Kapathi and others have expressed. Like, yep, it was right around end of November, early December.
text: o1，或者也可能是某个 GPT-5。更早还有一些 checkpoint，但我确实觉得有一种大致的共识可以参考，Kapathi 和其他人也表达过类似观点。对，差不多就是在 11 月底、12 月初那会儿。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Everyone who works at larger tech companies, it was the winter break because people just, you know, like, like the whole industry shuts down for two weeks, save for a few places where you're on call.
text: 凡是在大科技公司工作的人都知道，那正好是冬季假期，因为大家都会，你懂的，整个行业基本都会停摆两周，除了少数需要值班 on call 的地方。

speaker: Guest
text_en: But again, no production work happens across the industry. My sense was that people were playing with it because you give it your side projects you never finish and expect not to finish. And then they also got shocked. You're done.
text: 但总之，整个行业那段时间基本没有什么生产工作在推进。我的感觉是，大家当时都在拿它玩，因为你会把那些自己永远做不完、也默认做不完的 side project 丢给它。结果大家也都被震到了：居然做完了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And that was just a complete sort of break, right? Like if this was a movie, you'd hear the scratch sound. Like, you're like, wait, what? Rewind. What happened? I feel it was the most collective shock which happened individually.
text: 那感觉就像一次彻底的断裂，对吧？如果这是部电影，你会听到那种唱针刮盘的声音。你会想，等等，什么情况？倒回去。刚刚发生了什么？我觉得那是一次最集体性的震惊，只不过是每个人各自分别感受到的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And then people came back in January.
text: 然后到了 1 月，大家都回来上班了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And everyone, especially because a lot of the decision makers who are, you know, like CTOs, director of engineering, et cetera, were not as hands-on, but they were hands-on, and a lot of them, it's this weird thing where they came back and they started to mandate or like, say, all right, you guys need to use this because I've seen the future.
text: 然后所有人，尤其是很多决策者——比如 CTO、engineering director 之类的——虽然平时没那么亲自上手，但那次他们确实上手了。于是就出现了一种很奇怪的情况：他们回来之后开始强推，或者说直接要求，‘好，你们现在必须用这个，因为我已经看见未来了。’

speaker: Guest
text_en: I've literally used it. You need to see it.
text: 我是真的亲手用过。你们必须亲眼看看。

speaker: Host
text_en: So we're going back to a little bit of hardware, like people were trying to give, you know, like the new hardware into people's hands saying, you need to experience it because you're not going to believe it, right?
text: 所以我们又有点回到硬件那套了，就像以前大家会把新硬件塞到别人手里，说你得亲自体验一下，不然你根本不会相信，对吧？

speaker: Guest
text_en: There's something with this as well where you really don't believe it. We can talk about this and whoever's not tried it or not had that aha moment, I don't think we can convince them.
text: 这件事也有点像那样，你是真的不会相信。我们可以一直聊这个，但凡是没试过、还没到那个“啊哈”时刻的人，我觉得我们是说服不了他们的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: This is another one of those cases where words just are not effective. You need to sit down in front of OpenCode or whatever harness that you use, Use one of the frontier models. Start with that. Start with Opus. I'd say start with Opus.
text: 这又是那种光靠语言根本没用的情况。你得亲自坐到 OpenCode 或者你用的其他工具前面，用一个前沿模型。先从这个开始，先从 Opus 开始。要我说，就先用 Opus。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It's the best frontier model. Other models are better at other things. Blah, blah.
text: 它是最好的前沿模型。别的模型在别的事情上可能更强。诸如此类吧。

speaker: Guest
text_en: But if you're just going to work on a piece of code and you want to see what the current frontier is, and if you, I mean, I'd be shocked if any of your listeners haven't done it already, but if there should be some left, now is the time.
text: 但如果你只是想处理一段代码，想看看当前最前沿已经到什么程度了，而且如果你——我是说，要是你的听众里居然还有人没试过，我会很惊讶——但如果真还有，那现在就是时候了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And I don't want you to say in the sense, I found it really off-putting, this trend on X, where unless you've internalized everything there is about AI, like you've been left behind, shut up. First of all, patently not true.
text: 而且我不希望你们有那种感觉，就是我很反感 X 上现在这个风气：好像除非你已经把 AI 的一切都内化了，不然你就落伍了，就闭嘴。首先，这显然不是真的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: You could literally pick up everything in the next three weeks. This is the other magical thing about this kind of project, right? Or progress.
text: 你完全可以在接下来的三周里把这些都补起来。这也是这类项目——或者说这种进展——另一个神奇的地方，对吧？

speaker: Guest
text_en: When, if we'd been having this conversation in spring of last year, everyone had been like, MCPs, MCPs, MCPs. And do you know what? You can now manage to just have jumped over that entire thing and go straight to CLIs and skills.
text: 如果我们是在去年春天聊这个，所有人都会说 MCP、MCP、MCP。你知道吗？你现在完全可以直接跳过那整套东西，直接上 CLI 和 skills。

speaker: Guest
text_en: That's just worth having in mind that this FOMO, that unless you're up on all of it as it happens play-by-play, you're left behind, is complete and utter nonsense. That being said, I can still appreciate that some people were early.
text: 这个得记在心里：那种 FOMO，觉得如果你不能在事情发生的当下、一步不落地全程跟进，就会被甩在后面，这种想法完全是无稽之谈。不过话说回来，我也还是承认，有些人确实看得更早。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And for me, Toby Lutke at Shopify is the main individual who saw this and saw the changes that were coming from it way earlier than I did and have really helped drag me into this by constantly sending me like, Hey, you look at this, look at this, look at this.
text: 对我来说，Shopify 的 Toby Lutke 就是那个关键人物。他比我早得多就看到了这件事，也看到了它将带来的变化，而且他一直不停地给我发消息，像是在说，嘿，你看看这个，看看这个，再看看这个，硬是把我一步步拉了进来。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And I do think that's actually quite helpful. It's quite helpful to be surrounded by people who have a higher faith or maybe their eyes are a little further up. Like my eyes tend to be relatively close to the road, like right in front of me.
text: 我确实觉得这非常有帮助。身边有一些信念更强、或者说眼光看得更远的人，真的很有帮助。像我自己的视线通常就比较贴着路面，只看眼前这一小段。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And some people have a gaze that's a little higher up and sometimes they see things that don't come to pass. In this case, Toby saw exactly where we're going two years ago. And I finally saw it because the road came to me in December.
text: 而有些人的目光会抬得更高一点，所以他们有时能看到一些东西，当然也不一定都会成真。但这次，Toby 在两年前就准确看到了我们要去的方向。而我直到去年 12 月，等这条路真正走到我面前时，才终于看清。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And it's funny because along the way, I kept saying like, Yep, when the models get good enough, when they can do all these things, it's going to be amazing.
text: 挺有意思的是，这一路上我一直都在说，没错，等模型足够好了，等它们能做到这些事的时候，那一定会非常惊人。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And thinking, wow, it's going to be, I don't know, 18 months, two years, maybe it's five years. It's very hard to predict these inflection points. And I think the industry itself didn't even predict the inflection point, right?
text: 但我当时心里想的是，哇，这可能还要 18 个月、两年，也许甚至五年。这种拐点真的很难预测。我觉得就连这个行业本身，当时也没预测到这个拐点，对吧？

speaker: Guest
text_en: You have an entire city, Silicon Valley and surrounding areas, San Fran, focused on making this happen. But predicting exactly when the hockey stick starts hockeying is very difficult. But then it happened. And now my daily work is very different.
text: 整个城市，Silicon Valley 以及周边地区、San Fran，都在全力推动这件事发生。但要准确预测这条曲线什么时候会开始像冰球杆那样陡然上扬，是非常困难的。不过后来它真的发生了。现在我每天的工作已经完全不一样了。

speaker: Host
text_en: So what is your daily work now? My daily work is agent first on everything. Going agent first is a good time to mention our season sponsor, Sonar. When shifting to agent first work, one thing that inherently comes up is the quality of the code.
text: 那你现在每天的工作是什么样？我现在的日常工作，所有事情都是 agent first。说到转向 agent first 的工作方式，也正好可以提一下我们这一季的赞助商 Sonar。转向 agent first 工作流时，一个必然会冒出来的问题，就是代码质量。

speaker: Host
text_en: Sonar, the makers of SonarCube, is deeply rooted in the core belief that code quality and code security are inherently linked. High quality code is naturally more resilient.
text: Sonar，也就是 SonarCube 的开发公司，深信一个核心理念：代码质量和代码安全本质上是紧密相连的。高质量的代码天然就更有韧性。

speaker: Host
text_en: And as agents start writing code at a massive scale, that verification layer becomes your most important security parameter. This is where solutions like SonarCube Advanced Security are valuable.
text: 而随着 agent 开始大规模写代码，这层验证机制就成了你最重要的安全参数。这也正是 SonarCube Advanced Security 这类解决方案有价值的地方。

speaker: Host
text_en: With this new malicious package detection, Advanced Security provides a real-time circuit breaker, automatically stopping agents from pulling in unverified or risky third-party libraries before they ever hit your pipeline.
text: 借助这项新的恶意软件包检测功能，Advanced Security 提供了一个实时断路器，能在 agent 把未经验证或有风险的第三方库拉进你的流水线之前，自动把它拦下来。

speaker: Host
text_en: The impact is measurable too. Developers who verify their code with Sonar are 44% less likely to report experiencing outages due to AI, as per Sonar's State of Code Developer Survey 2026 report.
text: 而且它的效果也是可量化的。根据 Sonar 的《2026 代码开发者现状调查》报告，使用 Sonar 验证代码的开发者，报告因 AI 导致宕机的可能性要低 44%。

speaker: Host
text_en: It's really about closing the gap between the speed of AI and the reality of production security. What else is Sonar doing to help reduce outages, improve security, and lower risk associated with AI and agenda coding? com slash pragmatic to find out.
text: 归根结底，这就是在弥合 AI 速度和生产环境安全现实之间的差距。Sonar 还在做些什么，来帮助减少宕机、提升安全性，并降低与 AI 和 agent 编码相关的风险？前往 sonar.com/pragmatic 了解更多。

speaker: Host
text_en: With this, let's get back to David's agent-first workflow. So specifically CloudCode? Or OpenCode? OpenCode is my main harness. I also use CloudCode a little bit. They, unfortunately, got that early lead. Opus is currently the best model.
text: 好，接下来我们回到 David 这种 agent-first 的工作流。所以你具体用的是 CloudCode？还是 OpenCode？OpenCode 是我的主力 harness。我也会稍微用一点 CloudCode。很遗憾，他们确实抢到了先发优势。Opus 目前是最好的模型。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So then they started thinking a little bit in that like the game is single match instead of thinking it's multiple rounds and yanked their subscription from OpenCode.
text: 所以后来他们的思路就有点变成了，把这场游戏当成单局对决，而不是多轮博弈，然后就把 OpenCode 的订阅给撤掉了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So if you wanna use your Mac subscription, you kind of have to use their harness, which I don't love it. I think it's a mistake, but leave that be for a second. And let's just celebrate the fact that they have the best model. And Opus...
text: 所以如果你想用他们的 Mac 订阅，基本上就只能用他们自己的 harness，这点我不太喜欢。我觉得这是个错误，不过这个先放一边。我们先承认一个事实：他们现在的模型就是最好的。至于 Opus……

speaker: Guest
text_en: Fiz for, 45, 46 is also nice, but 45 to me was the inflection point. And it creates a lot of competition because everyone wants to catch up and overtake them now. Of course. And especially because you see Anthropic's revenues.
text: Fiz 到了 45、46 也挺不错，但对我来说，45 才是那个拐点。而且这会带来很多竞争，因为现在所有人都想追上他们、再超过他们。当然了。尤其是你看到 Anthropic 的营收。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I think start of the year, they're at $9 billion. A few weeks later, they're at like whatever, $14. Now they're at $19 or something.
text: 我记得年初的时候，他们大概是 90 亿美元。几周之后，就到了差不多 140 亿。现在又到 190 亿左右了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It's just the craziest rocket ship you could possibly imagine, which is Inspiring all this capital to be deployed for competitors and so forth. Which is wonderful. Great to see.
text: 这简直就是你能想象到最疯狂的火箭式增长，也因此激励了大量资本投向竞争对手之类的方向。这很好，真挺让人高兴的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So even if I don't love everything that they do and Claude Code is not my preferred harness, manage to hold two things in your head at the same time.
text: 所以就算我不是很喜欢他们做的所有事情，而且 Claude Code 也不是我首选的工具链，我还是会尽量同时接受两件事并存。

speaker: Guest
text_en: This is what I also try to do even with Apple, which I have serious griefs about how they operate and act as the gatekeeper and all the other nonsense we've talked about.
text: 我对 Apple 也是这样。我对他们的运作方式、他们充当守门人的做法，还有我们聊过的那些其他乱七八糟的问题，确实有很多强烈不满。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And then I also keep my, I just love computers hat on and go, I like the new Neo. I might even buy a new Neo. And just see what is possible at $500. For Opus, I have no qualms about using Opus.
text: 但与此同时，我也会戴上那顶“我就是喜欢电脑”的帽子，然后说，我喜欢新的 Neo。我甚至可能会买一台新的 Neo。就想看看 500 美元这个价位能做到什么。至于 Opus，我用起来完全没心理负担。

speaker: Guest
text_en: In fact, whenever I feel like, this is a really hard problem, I go to Opus right now. But I also use other models. And one of the things I've incorporated into my flow is to kind of have two models going at the same time at different speeds.
text: 事实上，只要我觉得“这问题真的很难”，我现在就会去用 Opus。但我也会用别的模型。而且我已经把一个做法融入到自己的工作流里了，就是同时跑两个模型，只是速度不同。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So I use TMUX and I have this layout thing that's built into Amachi where it'll start my new Vim editor on the left side and then it'll start two panes on the right side. On the top is OpenCode running KimiK25.
text: 所以我会用 TMUX，我在 Amachi 里做了个布局功能：它会在左边启动我新的 Vim 编辑器，然后在右边开两个窗格。上面那个是运行 KimiK25 的 OpenCode。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And on the bottom is Opus running in CloudCode. And then at the very bottom, I have a strip of terminal. And almost everything I started in one of the agents. And I tell them what I want. Then I hop over to Neovim.
text: 最下面是跑在 CloudCode 里的 Opus。再往最底下，我留了一条终端窗口。几乎所有东西我都是先在某个 agent 里启动起来。我告诉它们我想要什么，然后我再切到 Neovim。

speaker: Guest
text_en: First I do a SpaceGG to look at the LacyGit diff on it. What is it changing? If it looks correct, I'll just commit. We're done. Great. And then sometimes it doesn't look correct and I'll go in and alter the code myself.
text: 我先按一下 SpaceGG，看看上面的 LacyGit diff。它到底改了什么？如果看起来没问题，我就直接 commit。完事了，很棒。然后有时候看起来不对，我就会进去自己改代码。

speaker: Guest
text_en: But the ratio and how quickly the ratio changes is still astounding. I went from early November last year, I'm code first everything. I started the editor, I'll spend whatever long it is.
text: 但这个比例，以及这个比例变化得有多快，还是让人很震撼。去年 11 月初的时候，我还是凡事都先自己写代码。我会打开编辑器，花多久都行。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And then at some point, if I get stuck or if I want a second opinion, I'll go ask my friendly Flanker to give me a second opinion. That's just not how it is anymore. Now I start with the agent. Now it'll give me the draft.
text: 然后到了某个时候，如果我卡住了，或者想听听第二意见，我才会去问我那个友好的 Flanker，让它给我点第二意见。但现在已经完全不是这么回事了。现在我是先从 agent 开始。它会先给我一个草稿。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I'll review the draft and I'll make alterations if need be. And then, just recently, I flipped it even further. So we're working on a CLI for Basecamp, so we can get full agent accessibility for Basecamp. It's astounding.
text: 我会审一遍这个草稿，有需要的话再做修改。而且就在最近，我又把这个流程往前推了一步。我们现在在给 Basecamp 做一个 CLI，这样就能让 agent 完整访问 Basecamp。真的很惊人。

speaker: Guest
text_en: First, actually, let me rewind. As soon as I got palled on how good the agents were and how capable they were, I immediately tried to raise my gaze up towards the end of the road and think, do we even need MCP? Do we even need CLIs?
text: 不过先等等，让我倒回去一点。就在我真正意识到这些 agent 有多厉害、多有能力之后，我立刻开始把视线抬到更远的终点去想：我们真的还需要 MCP 吗？我们真的还需要 CLI 吗？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Do we even need anything? Can't the agent just figure it all out? This was when I installed OpenClaw. So I installed OpenClaw on a VM and I thought, what should I do here? Let's see how far we can push it and what it can do by itself.
text: 我们到底还需要任何东西吗？难道 agent 不能自己把一切都搞定吗？那时候我装了 OpenClaw。于是我在一台 VM 上装了 OpenClaw，然后我想，我该拿它做点什么呢？看看我们到底能把它推到多远，看看它自己能做到什么程度。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So I thought, I want this claw in Basecamp. I want this claw in Fizzy. Let me just try to invite it as if it was a human. So I just wrote it. Can you sign up for Fizzy? I'm not giving you any tools. I'm not giving you any MCP.
text: 所以我就想，我想把这个 claw 放进 Basecamp。我想把这个 claw 放进 Fizzy。那我干脆就试试，像邀请一个真人一样去邀请它。所以我就直接写给它：你能注册一下 Fizzy 吗？我不给你任何工具，也不给你任何 MCP。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I'm not giving you a CLI. do. Go sign up. And you see it chuck along. And then, yeah, I've signed up, but it's asking for an email address. Or I'm trying to sign up. It's asking for an email address. I'm like, oh, yeah, right.
text: 我没给你什么 CLI。去，自己去注册。然后你就看着它一路咔咔往前跑。接着，嗯，我已经点了注册，但它在要一个邮箱地址。或者说，我正准备注册，它在要一个邮箱地址。我就想，哦，对哦。

speaker: Guest
text_en: You need an email address. An agent doesn't have an email address. com. I'm like, it's going to fail this one. And it's chuck, chuck, chuck. com. Here's the password. Write it down somewhere safe. I'm now also signed up for Fizzy.
text: 你得有个邮箱地址。agent 又没有邮箱地址。我就想，这下要卡住了。结果它还是咔咔咔继续。它说，这是邮箱，这是密码，记到安全的地方。我现在还顺手给它注册了 Fizzy。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I got the confirmation email in my inbox. We're all good. What do you want me to do? I'm like, what? Are you telling me that you could one shot signing up through a browser to these things? Now, maybe that shouldn't be surprising.
text: 确认邮件我都已经在收件箱里收到了，一切正常。你还想让我做什么？我当时就想，啊？你的意思是，你居然能一把过，直接通过浏览器把这些都注册完？当然，也许这本来就不算太让人意外。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Maybe that was already possible with Sonnet 3 or one of the early models. I don't know.
text: 也许用 Sonnet 3 或者更早期的一些模型，这就已经能做到了。我也不确定。

speaker: Guest
text_en: But when you experience it yourself on your own damn claw that you're just telling over Telegram to do something and it's signing up for products autonomously, that's pretty startling. It was for me.
text: 但当你亲自在自己那只该死的 claw 上体验到这件事——你只是在 Telegram 里吩咐它做点什么，它就自己去注册产品了——那真的挺震撼的。至少对我是这样。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And then the next time I went, like, well, if it can sign up for Hay and it can sign up for Fizzy, let me invite it to Basecamp. So I send it an invitation to its own email address. Here's the invitation link to Basecamp.
text: 然后下一次我就想，既然它能注册 Hay，也能注册 Fizzy，那我干脆邀请它进 Basecamp 吧。于是我给它自己的邮箱地址发了一封邀请。这是 Basecamp 的邀请链接。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Can you just jump into the AI Labs project that we have and introduce yourself to the team? Hey, I'm David's assistant. It's very nice to meet you all. I've read back the transcript a little bit. I see you're all excited about these things.
text: 你能直接进我们那个 AI Labs 项目，跟团队打个招呼吗？“嗨，我是 David 的助手，很高兴认识大家。我稍微回看了一下聊天记录，看得出来你们都对这些东西很兴奋。”

speaker: Guest
text_en: And you just go again. What? What? And that was fun because it showed me that even if it was going to take a while, it did take a while. It took a while. This is agent terms. It took, I don't know, seven minutes.
text: 然后你又会再次愣住：什么？什么？这事很有意思，因为它让我看到，就算它确实要花点时间——也的确花了点时间——按 agent 的标准来说，我不知道，大概七分钟吧。

speaker: Guest
text_en: That was like, oh, it feels like an eternity. But it was able. We've built to do it, and that seems like the end state. The end state is that agents will not need any of our accommodations. They do not need any on-ramp.
text: 那感觉就像，哇，简直漫长得像过了一个世纪。但它是可行的。我们已经把东西构建到能做到这一点了，而那看起来就是最终状态。最终状态就是，agent 不再需要我们提供任何特殊适配，也不需要任何上手引导。

speaker: Guest
text_en: They're not coming on a little wheelchair. They'll be coming on bionic legs and running five times as fast as you in about two seconds, which we'll get to in a second too, the speed aspect of it.
text: 它们不是坐着个小轮椅进场的。它们会踩着仿生腿进来，差不多两秒钟后就能跑得比你快五倍，这点我们等下也会讲到，就是它的速度这一面。

speaker: Guest
text_en: But then you also realize, okay, well, I can't just sit around fiddling my thumbs until AGI happens. Let's build for today. And that's what we've been building for Basecamp. We've been building the CLI. We're going to build it for Hey.
text: 但你也会意识到，好吧，我也不能就这么坐着干等 AGI 发生。还是先为当下去构建吧。这也是我们一直在为 Basecamp 做的事。我们一直在做 CLI，接下来也会给 Hey 做。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We're going to build it for Fizzy. We're going to build it for everything, even probably some of the legacy products.
text: 我们会给 Fizzy 做，也会给所有东西做，甚至可能连一些老产品也会做。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And what I love about the CLIs, as much as I also love it about these harnesses, is that they validated the fundamental Unix philosophy from like whatever, 71.
text: 我特别喜欢 CLI 的一点，就像我也很喜欢这些 harness 一样，是它们验证了大概从 71 年开始的那个 Unix 基本哲学。

speaker: Guest
text_en: You should just build small tools that can interoperate with pipes, and you can put things together. That's the Unix philosophy, right? It's the total Unix philosophy. And that is actually the magic to me about seeing everything having a CLI.
text: 你就该构建一些能通过管道互相协作的小工具，然后把它们拼起来。这就是 Unix 哲学，对吧？这完全就是 Unix 哲学。而对我来说，看到所有东西都有 CLI，真正神奇的地方就在这儿。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It's not that Basecamp is easier to use now with the CLI. No, no. It's that GitHub also has a CLI. And Sentry, I don't know if they have a CLI, but they have an MCP that you can tie all these things together.
text: 并不是说 Basecamp 现在有了 CLI 就更容易用了。不是，不是。关键在于 GitHub 也有 CLI。还有 Sentry，我不知道他们有没有 CLI，但他们有 MCP，这样你就能把这些东西全都串起来。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And now you can tell an agent, hey, we have some errors in Sentry. Can you go check them out? Then post a write-up to Basecamp iterating what's wrong. Then go on GitHub, come up with a pull request, post a comment back to Basecamp when you're done.
text: 这样一来，你就可以对一个 agent 说，嘿，Sentry 里有一些报错，你能去看一下吗？然后把哪里出了问题整理成一份说明，发到 Basecamp 上。接着再去 GitHub，提一个 pull request，做完之后再回到 Basecamp 留个评论。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And now we have a central record in Basecamp where we're following the work as it's going on while we have an agent doing work, looking things up. And again, when we try to talk about it and relay it, I guess some people can see it.
text: 现在我们在 Basecamp 里有了一份中心记录，在 agent 一边干活、一边查资料的时候，我们也能同步跟进整个工作过程。再说一次，当我们试着去讲这件事、把它转述给别人时，我想有些人是能看明白的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And now OpenAI has enough videos on YouTube and so forth, so you can get at least a passenger ride. But try it yourself with your own product, with your own tasks, and with your own prompts. And you will be pilled.
text: 现在 OpenAI 在 YouTube 之类的平台上已经有足够多的视频了，所以你至少可以先搭个便车感受一下。但你要用你自己的产品、你自己的任务、你自己的 prompts 亲自试试。你一试就会被彻底洗脑。

speaker: Guest
text_en: You will be simultaneously incredibly excited for what we've been able to make sand do, the silicon, the chips, the weights, the whole thing. And then also a little bit anxious about where it's all going to go.
text: 你会一边对我们居然能让沙子干出这些事感到无比兴奋——硅、芯片、权重，整套东西都太惊人了；另一边又会对这一切最终会走向哪里有点焦虑。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And it's in that tension that I, and probably anyone else who's been pilled on this, live, right? Wait a minute, if we're already here, what does 18 months from now look like?
text: 而我——大概还有所有被这东西震住的人——就活在这种张力里，对吧？等一下，如果我们现在都已经到这一步了，那 18 个月后会是什么样？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Like, if in the last 3 months, we've upended my entire understanding of what's possible with computers, what's the next 3 months look like? What do the next 9 months look like?
text: 比如说，如果过去 3 个月已经彻底颠覆了我对计算机能力边界的理解，那接下来的 3 个月会是什么样？接下来的 9 个月又会是什么样？

speaker: Host
text_en: Yeah, this is where, like, I was a little bit on your end for a long time. And I think I still am, where I believe what works. And I'm always skeptical of projections. Moore's law broke down at some point.
text: 对，这就是我之前很长一段时间里，其实有点站在你这边的地方。而且我觉得现在某种程度上也还是。我相信那些已经被验证有效的东西。我一直都对各种预测持怀疑态度。Moore 定律在某个时候确实失效了。

speaker: Host
text_en: I lived through when everyone said it will continue forever. And you know, and then it broke as we all suspected it would. But then it found another way. It's the good point about the Moore's law, right? It broke for individual cores.
text: 我经历过那个人人都说它会永远持续下去的时代。然后你也知道，后来它还是像我们很多人预料的那样失效了。但接着它又找到了另一条路。这也是 Moore 定律有意思的地方，对吧？它是在单核层面失效的。

speaker: Host
text_en: How much can you push it? And then we just went, well, what if you just had, what's the latest chip? 256 on the AMD Zen chips, right? And even when performance broke, we went into power consumption and size and all of those things.
text: 单核还能被你推到多远？然后我们就转而想，那如果你干脆堆更多核呢？现在最新的芯片是什么来着？AMD Zen 芯片上已经到 256 了，对吧？甚至在性能提升这条路走不动之后，我们又转向了功耗、尺寸，以及所有这些方向。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So yeah, but it's harder for me to also just to say, oh, it's going to stop here because we've seen it grow. We know the approaches that they're taking, this larger and larger training sets. And it's been working so far.
text: 所以，是啊，但我也很难直接说，哦，它会停在这里，因为我们已经看到它在增长。我们知道他们在采用什么方法，训练集越来越大。而且到目前为止，这套方法一直是有效的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And there's also the bitter lesson, which I think is a, it's such a short paper that is just so worth reading. I think it's one of probably the most popular papers outside of academic circles.
text: 还有就是《The Bitter Lesson》，我觉得那真是一篇特别值得读的文章，虽然很短。我觉得它大概是学术圈外最受欢迎的论文之一。

speaker: Host
text_en: Yes, because it just lays out this thing that we don't want to believe that. We want to believe that our knowledge, our understanding is superior.
text: 对，因为它就是把一件我们不愿意相信的事讲清楚了。我们总想相信，我们的知识、我们的理解是更高明的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: That, you know, you and me knowing how to code or me putting in these 15 years or however long it's been, it's special. Sometimes it shows that it's not as special.
text: 就是说，你我会写代码，或者我投入了这15年，不管具体多久吧，这很特别。但有时候现实会说明，它其实没那么特别。

speaker: Guest
text_en: What's interesting actually is like right this second, this snapshot in time, it a little bit is. And this is a funny purification that's happening junior versus senior developer. 6 has been from the most senior people.
text: 但有意思的是，就在此时此刻，从这个时间点来看，它又确实有那么一点特别。现在正在发生一种很有意思的分化：初级开发者和高级开发者之间的差别。很多价值其实都来自那些最资深的人。

speaker: Guest
text_en: The people who are able to validate whether what the agent produces is suitable to be deployed to millions of people. There was just this story yesterday about some of the major outages at Amazon. Yep.
text: 也就是那些能够判断 agent 产出的东西，是否适合部署给几百万用户的人。昨天刚有个新闻，说 Amazon 出现了一些重大故障。对。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And Amazon's own internal analysis essentially pinned that. We can no longer let junior programmers ship agent-generated code to production without review.
text: 而 Amazon 自己的内部分析基本上把原因指向了这一点：我们不能再让初级程序员在没有审核的情况下，把 agent 生成的代码直接发布到生产环境。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And the problem with that is, first of all, I think that's the realization most companies are now having across the industry. Whenever it's mission-critical for something of that nature, we cannot yet rely on the agents to have vetted it all.
text: 问题在于，首先，我觉得这其实是现在整个行业里大多数公司都在意识到的一件事。只要是那种任务关键型、性质类似的场景，我们还不能依赖 agent 说它们已经把一切都审查妥当了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And junior programmers are not capable of figuring it out. Therefore, the role is suddenly more tenuous than it was six, nine months ago. Because a senior programmer can. And this is why senior programmers are getting so much more acceleration.
text: 而初级程序员并没有能力把这些问题理清楚。所以相比六个月、九个月前，这个岗位一下子变得更不稳了。因为高级程序员可以做到。这也是为什么高级程序员现在获得了大得多的加速。

speaker: Guest
text_en: They're able to, first of all, work in parallel with lots of agents, but critically examine the quality of the agent output and have a high degree of confidence of whether this is going to work or not, or redirect them if not. Because this...
text: 首先，他们能够同时和很多 agent 并行协作；但更关键的是，他们能严格审视 agent 输出的质量，并且高度确信这东西到底能不能跑起来；如果不行，也能及时把方向纠正过来。因为这……

speaker: Guest
text_en: This is what made them senior in the first place. This was the role that they had, that they had the long insight and history and overview of the architecture. How does it all fit in? Is this going to work? Is this not going to work?
text: 这本来就是他们之所以能成为高级程序员的原因。这就是他们原本承担的角色：他们有长期积累下来的洞察、经验，以及对整体架构的全局视角。所有东西是怎么拼在一起的？这能行吗？那不能行吗？

speaker: Guest
text_en: This was the role they played to junior programmers, but now they can play that role to agents. And agents are faster at following instructions and redirections, and suddenly you have senior developers who can 5X, 10X their individual productivity.
text: 这原本是他们面对初级程序员时扮演的角色，但现在他们可以把这个角色用在 agent 身上。而且 agent 在执行指令和根据反馈调整方向这件事上更快，于是你突然就有了能把个人生产力提升到 5 倍、10 倍的高级开发者。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And now, this is the second order effect. If you manage to 5X or 10X a senior developer, that person's value per hour just went up 10X. Now, take that hour.
text: 然后，这就是第二层效应。如果你真的能把一个高级开发者放大到 5 倍或 10 倍，那这个人每小时的价值就直接涨了 10 倍。现在，再看这一个小时。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Instead of that person spending it with the agents just shipping stuff and making things better, they spend that hour as they would before teaching a junior human how to do things better.
text: 这个人不再是把这一个小时花在和 agent 一起不断交付东西、把东西做得更好上，而是会像以前一样，把这一个小时花在教一个初级人类员工怎么把事情做得更好。

speaker: Guest
text_en: There's something in that equation that's in play right now, and it's not clear how it's going to map out. Now, one way it could map out is that the agents will get so good that they stop making mistakes.
text: 这个等式里现在有个变量正在起作用，但最后会怎么演化，还不清楚。一种可能的演化方向是，agent 会变得非常厉害，厉害到不再犯错。

speaker: Guest
