text_en: They become senior in their capacity to ship working code. This is what my bet would be if we look X amount of time forward because this is what just happened with cars. So self-driving Teslas now drive better than humans do.
text: 它们在交付可运行代码这件事上的能力，会变得像高级程序员一样。如果把时间往后拉长来看，这是我会下注的方向，因为汽车领域刚刚就发生了同样的事。所以现在自动驾驶的 Tesla，开得已经比人类更好了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Not all humans, not in all circumstances, but on average.
text: 也不是所有人、所有情况下都这样，但平均来看是这样。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It's very possible that if we're able to delegate the mortal risk, the highest criticality we basically deal with on a daily basis sitting in a metal tube along other metal tubes that go 60 miles an hour where you can die if someone makes a mistake, we delegate that to an agent.
text: 很有可能，如果我们能把这种致命风险——也就是我们日常面对的最高级别风险——交给一个 agent 来处理，像是坐在一个金属管子里，旁边还有别的金属管子，以每小时 60 英里的速度跑，只要有人犯个错你就可能没命。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Well, they can probably figure out how to make the code work too. Right? So I do think it's coming, but who knows when, who knows how. Right now, we're at a stage where the bulk of the benefits are accruing to the most senior developers.
text: 那他们大概也能想办法把代码搞定，对吧？所以我确实觉得这会到来，只是不知道是什么时候、会以什么方式到来。现在这个阶段，大部分收益还是落在最资深的开发者身上。

speaker: Host
text_en: And also, I wonder, just like with self-driving, you realize there's always caveats. So, for example, inside companies where it matters, when you're a startup, you have zero customers, it doesn't matter.
text: 而且我也在想，就像自动驾驶一样，你会发现总是有各种前提和限制。比如说，在公司内部、在这件事真的重要的场景里；如果你是个 startup，一个客户都没有，那就无所谓。

speaker: Host
text_en: You can one-shot it and it doesn't matter if it doesn't work and it crashes. But inside these companies at Uber, I just got details on how they're adopting AI and they have all these tools, Cloud Code and all of these things.
text: 你可以一次性直接生成，哪怕跑不起来、崩了也没关系。但在 Uber 这种公司内部，我刚了解到他们采用 AI 的一些细节，他们有各种工具，Cloud Code 之类的，还有其他这些东西。

speaker: Host
text_en: But what we realized as well, when you just put it in there, they have all these internal monorepos. They have their ticketing systems. They have their Slack.
text: 但我们也意识到，当你真把它放进去时，他们有各种内部 monorepo，还有他们的工单系统、他们的 Slack。

speaker: Host
text_en: They have their RFCs, design documents on how and why they have this jumble of a mess with microservices, which was a fun way that we originally connected like many years ago.
text: 他们还有 RFC、设计文档，里面写着他们为什么、以及怎么搞出了这堆乱糟糟的微服务——这也是很多年前我们最初建立联系时聊得很开心的一个点。

speaker: Host
text_en: But what they found is they built a bunch of internal systems, a lot of it, to help to feed into these agent harnesses and now they're working better.
text: 但他们后来发现，他们搭了很多内部系统，其中很大一部分就是为了给这些 agent harness 提供输入，现在效果确实更好了。

speaker: Host
text_en: But this where we are right now is, and this is why if you're a senior engineer and one of these companies or a staff engineer at like Uber, and you move to Google, suddenly you're not going to be as valuable or as efficient for a while until you learn all the systems.
text: 但我们现在的情况就是这样，这也是为什么如果你是一家这类公司的资深工程师，或者像 Uber 那样的 staff engineer，你跳去 Google，突然之间你在一段时间内就不会那么有价值、也不会那么高效，直到你把那边所有系统都学会。

speaker: Host
text_en: So I wonder if just like with self-driving, you know, self-driving works great. I was in SF and in LA and Waymos, they drive so nice. Like... My Teslas are driving in LA, driving us to the airport every time. The whole family.
text: 所以我就在想，这是不是有点像自动驾驶。你知道，自动驾驶确实很好用。我在 SF 和 LA 坐过 Waymo，它们开得特别稳。像……我的 Tesla 在 LA 也会自己开，每次都把我们送到机场，全家一起。

speaker: Host
text_en: I sit peacefully, watch the road, but do not steer at all on that entire journey. Well, except my Waymo got stuck because a truck was parking on a narrow street and a car had a bike shed. And I knew that it should not go there, but it didn't know.
text: 我就安安静静坐着，看着路，但整段路程我完全不用碰方向盘。嗯，除了有一次我的 Waymo 被卡住了，因为一辆卡车在一条很窄的街上停车，旁边还有辆车边上有个自行车棚。我知道它不该往那边走，但它自己不知道。

speaker: Host
text_en: So a human operator came in. But anyway, but even with Waymos, you know, like there's things like they drive in pretty good weather. They've been mapped out.
text: 所以后来有人类远程操作员介入了。不过话说回来，即便是 Waymo，你也知道，它们通常也是在天气比较好的情况下开，而且路线都是提前测绘过的。

speaker: Host
text_en: So I wonder if in software engineering, I wonder if this has these parallels where we have all of, you know, like these companies have their specialized landscape.
text: 所以我就在想，在软件工程里是不是也有类似的平行关系，就是这些公司都有自己非常专门化的环境。

speaker: Host
text_en: And once you map it, once you do all the tools, once you figure out these things and with self-driving, it took it took 10 years, right?
text: 一旦你把它测绘清楚了，把各种工具都做好了，把这些东西都摸透了——而自动驾驶做到这一步可是花了 10 年，对吧？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Like I was at Uber when they bought the self-driving thing, and we were hearing in the news that, you know, next year it's all gonna be over for drivers. And no.
text: 我当时就在 Uber，他们买下自动驾驶业务那会儿，我们在新闻里听到的说法是，你知道，明年司机这个职业就要完蛋了。结果并没有。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Yes, there are not going to be steering wheels anymore, which by the way, is an amazing anecdote because it just shows Elon's total faith in his mission. Because in 17 when he made that proclamation, it wasn't AI.
text: 是啊，当时还说以后连方向盘都不会有了。顺便说一句，这其实是个很惊人的小故事，因为它完全体现了 Elon 对自己使命的那种绝对信念。因为他在 17 年做出那个宣告的时候，那还不是 AI。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It was 500,000 lines of hand-coded C++. Right? Like that model was never, ever going to get us to the full self-driving, but he had just total faith in the vision. And then eventually, hey, here long comes AI and it's so good.
text: 那可是 50 万行手写的 C++。对吧？那套模型根本、绝对不可能把我们带到完全自动驾驶，但他就是对这个愿景有着十足的信念。后来 AI 终于来了，而且强得离谱。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And if you train it on billions of hours of road use, it actually can do it and it can do it better than most humans. In fact, I'm a pretty good driver. I'd like to say.
text: 如果你用几十亿小时的道路驾驶数据去训练它，它其实真的能做到，而且能比大多数人开得更好。说真的，我觉得我自己已经算是个挺不错的司机了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I'm not the best chauffeur because my, I don't know, impatience have a tendency to provoke the throttle. That's not always as pleasant for passengers as it is fun for me.
text: 我不是最好的专职司机，因为我这人吧，我也不知道，性子有点急，脚下容易忍不住给油。这对我来说很爽，但对乘客来说未必总是那么舒服。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And when I let the Tesla autopilot drive, it's just the best chauffeur in the world. It's just perfectly calm. Better than you. Better than me. Better than the queen's chauffeur, I think. It's throttle actuation and deceleration. is godlike.
text: 但当我让 Tesla Autopilot 来开时，它简直就是世界上最好的专职司机。它完全沉着冷静。比你好。比我好。我觉得甚至比女王的司机还好。它对油门控制和减速的拿捏，简直像神一样。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It's actually AGI-like or ASI-like in its application within that narrow domain. And of course, when we get these anecdotes and these examples of holy smokes, it didn't take 10 years for the software.
text: 在那个狭窄领域里，它的实际表现已经有点像 AGI，甚至像 ASI 了。当然，当我们看到这些真实案例、这些让人惊掉下巴的例子时，就会觉得，天哪，软件根本没花 10 年。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It took 10 years from the proclamation, but what they were doing for seven of those years had nothing to do with what they're doing with FSD now because the FSD that's based on AI hadn't been running for that long.
text: 从最初宣告到现在确实过了 10 年，但其中有 7 年他们做的东西，和他们现在用在 FSD 上的东西根本不是一回事，因为基于 AI 的 FSD 并没有运行那么久。

speaker: Guest
text_en: 1, like the first version, you're like, wow, this is pretty good, but like, I better pay attention. 2. Over the course of 18 months, we went from, yeah, it's pretty good, but like, I'm going to pay attention here. To, why is there a steering wheel?
text: 1，也就是第一个版本时，你会觉得，哇，这已经挺不错了，但我最好还是盯着点。到了 2。在 18 个月里，我们从“嗯，挺不错，但这里我还是得注意”变成了“那为什么还需要方向盘？”

speaker: Guest
text_en: And that acceleration, that short period of time, of course, is something people look to when it comes to programming and go like, well, if we're here now and senior programmers still have to review it because otherwise you're going to get all your, whatever, four severity, eight downtimes at AWS because some AI pushed out some nonsense, what is it going to look like when they take the jump that FSD did over the same period of time?
text: 而这种加速度，这么短时间内的跃升，当然也会让人联想到编程。大家会想，如果我们现在还处在这个阶段，高级程序员还得继续做代码审查，不然 AWS 上就可能因为某个 AI 推出了一堆胡来东西，导致各种四级严重事故、8 次宕机之类的，那等它像 FSD 那样在同样时间里完成那种跳跃之后，又会是什么样子？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Now, I also think you can go completely crazy trying to just sit and soak in all of that. This is what I tried to do over the past year, go, I'm really excited for where this is going.
text: 不过我也觉得，如果你只是坐在那里拼命想把这一切都吸收进去，人很容易彻底疯掉。过去这一年我就试着这么做过，想着，我真的很期待这会发展到哪里。

speaker: Guest
text_en: But I'm also going to deal with what's possible today and what's enjoyable today and what we do right now. I'm not going to try to plan what my life looks like 12 months from now when maybe we do have AGI or we don't.
text: 但同时，我也会面对今天真正可能做到的事、今天真正让人享受的事，还有我们此刻正在做的事。我不会试图去规划 12 个月后的生活会是什么样，毕竟到那时也许我们已经有 AGI 了，也许还没有。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Now, there are other people who do that very well. I just watched an interview with Leopold on Drakesh from last year. He's thinking like, what does 2030 look like? What does the whatever 10 gigawatt data center look like?
text: 当然，也有些人特别擅长做这种思考。我刚看了去年 Leopold 在 Drakesh 上的一段采访。他想的是，2030 年会是什么样？那个什么 10 吉瓦的数据中心会是什么样？

speaker: Guest
text_en: I'm like, I'm very glad we have individuals who put thought into that because that's not my favorite spot to be. And I think most people are not that good at polishing the crystal ball.
text: 我会觉得，还好有一些人愿意去认真思考这些，因为那不是我最喜欢待的位置。而且我觉得，大多数人其实都不太擅长打磨水晶球、预测未来。

speaker: Host
text_en: No, well, I mean, this is a little bit unsettling as a software engineer in the sense of like, clearly this is where the industry wants to go. This is where a lot of effort will be put.
text: 对，不过我的意思是，作为软件工程师，这多少会让人有点不安，因为很明显，这就是整个行业想去的方向。很多投入都会砸在这上面。

speaker: Host
text_en: There will be a lot of businesses, software businesses built on this. A lot of VC money raised on this, by the way, who are going to tackle this and they will either like succeed or die. That's what the companies do.
text: 会有很多软件公司围绕这个建立起来。顺便说一句，也会有很多 VC 的钱投到这上面。这些公司会去攻这个方向，要么成功，要么死掉。公司就是这么运作的。

speaker: Host
text_en: But today, what you see it at 37signals with software engineers, you of course have mostly experienced engineers, although you did hire junior engineers as well. How is their kind of work changing? How is their satisfaction with work changing?
text: 但就今天来说，在 37signals，你看到软件工程师这边的情况是怎样的？当然，你们大多是有经验的工程师，不过你们也招了初级工程师。他们的工作方式是怎么变化的？他们对工作的满意度又是怎么变化的？

speaker: Host
text_en: Because that's also a thing, right? We keep arguing about like, is it making us more miserable, these things? Is it what we want to do? And how is it changing for you, right? I think it's... That's the biggest revelation, actually.
text: 因为这也是个问题，对吧？我们一直在争论，这些东西是不是让我们变得更痛苦了？这真的是我们想做的事吗？那对你来说又发生了什么变化，对吧？我觉得这其实才是……最大的启发。

speaker: Guest
text_en: More than even the capacity of the agents is my enjoyment running them. When I was on that last interview last summer, I was talking about, do you know what?
text: 比起这些 agents 的能力本身，更让我享受的是去驱动它们。去年夏天我参加最后那场面试时，我当时在想，你知道吗？

speaker: Guest
text_en: I don't want to be a project manager for agents because I had the mental model of a project manager of humans. And I thought, like, that's not what I enjoy. I don't want to be that far away from the production. I want to be in the mix.
text: 我不想做 agents 的项目经理，因为我脑子里对项目经理的理解，还是那种管理人的项目经理。我就觉得，那不是我喜欢的工作。我不想离实际产出那么远，我想在一线。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I want to have my hands in the code.
text: 我想亲手写代码。

speaker: Guest
text_en: What I failed to realize at the time was that running a bunch of agents feels less like being a project manager for agents and more like stepping into this super mech suit where suddenly I don't just have two arms.
text: 我当时没意识到的是，管理一群 agents 的感觉，并不像是在当 agents 的项目经理，反而更像是穿上了一套超级机甲，突然之间我不再只有两只手。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I have 12 and I can now look at seven screens at the same time, running five keyboards. I'm still the one doing it, even if I'm not typing this as a keyword in a program. I have been hyper-accelerated as a programmer.
text: 我有了 12 只手，还能同时看 7 个屏幕、操作 5 个键盘。事情还是我在做，只不过不是我亲自在程序里敲下这些关键词而已。作为程序员，我被极大加速了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It's a different kind of programmer, but it still has the same affinity to aesthetics, at least when I'm producing Ruby code. And I'm able to combine that while being vastly more productive on a bunch of things.
text: 这是一种不同类型的程序员，但它依然保有那种对美感的亲和力，至少在我产出 Ruby 代码的时候是这样。而且我还能把这一点和高得多的生产力结合起来，同时推进很多事情。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It's also like getting an incredible brain upgrade on even assessing issues. 4, I went into GitHub and we had, I don't know, 250 PRs pending. And I kind of just sighed a little bit and you're like, 250 PRs.
text: 这也像是连判断问题的能力都获得了一次惊人的大脑升级。有一次我打开 GitHub，看到我们有，怎么说呢，250 个 PR 还在 pending。我就轻轻叹了口气，心想，250 个 PR。

speaker: Guest
text_en: If I spend, I don't know, 15 minutes on each PR, how long is it going to take before I get to the end of it? And I thought, you know what? Let me try something else. Let me just try to ask Claude to, I'm not even doing anything with a system.
text: 如果我每个 PR 花个 15 分钟，不知道得多久才能全部看完。然后我想，你知道吗？我试点别的办法吧。我就想试着让 Claude 来——我甚至都还没配什么 system。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I just do review URL and the URL is the issue or is the PR. I was shocked. In 90 minutes, I think it was, I processed 100 PRs. And it wasn't that I merged all of them. In fact, I'd say I merged a small minority. Maybe 10% got merged as is.
text: 我就是看 review 的 URL，那个 URL 要么对应 issue，要么对应 PR。我当时都惊了。大概 90 分钟里，我处理了 100 个 PR。也不是说我把它们全都合并了。实际上，我会说真正原样合并的只占很小一部分，可能只有 10%。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Then maybe 20% got merged but with Claude's implementation. The programmer had correctly identified an issue but hand-rolled some code that I could see I didn't want to keep or sometimes I couldn't even see it.
text: 然后大概有 20% 是合并了，但用的是 Claude 的实现。也就是说，程序员确实正确识别出了一个问题，但自己手写了一些代码，我一看就知道不想留，有时候我甚至都看不太明白。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I just asked Claude and they said like, it's not quite right. And then I just asked Claude, But can you just cleanroom this? This the right problem? Let's fix it, but let's do it right. It would do it right away.
text: 我就去问 Claude，它会说，嗯，这个不太对。然后我再问 Claude，能不能直接按 clean room 的方式重写？问题方向是对的，那我们把它修掉，但要用正确的方式来做。它马上就能搞定。

speaker: Guest
text_en: In exactly the style as I would have written the rest of Amachi. Now, this isn't the high code of something, it's mostly just bash code.
text: 而且风格跟我自己写 Amachi 其余部分时会用的风格一模一样。当然，这不是什么特别高深的代码，基本上就是 bash 代码。

speaker: Guest
text_en: But there's still a shape to bash code and how you want it to look and can it feel coherent with the rest of the project? Agent's opus in this case, we just nail it.
text: 但 bash 代码也是有它的形状和风格的，你会希望它看起来是什么样，跟整个项目的其余部分放在一起是不是协调。在这个场景里，Agent 的 opus 版本就是拿捏得特别准。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And then the second half of it was split between 25% things I then just realized, I just don't want this, it shouldn't, we shouldn't have it. And 25% Claude telling me, maybe there's something here, but it's a really not a good implementation.
text: 然后另一半大概分成两类：25% 是我后来意识到，我根本就不想要这个，这东西就不该存在；还有 25% 是 Claude 告诉我，这里面也许有点东西，但实现真的不行。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We don't have a straight shot to making a great one. A hundred issues in 90 minutes. And I sat back, this would have been a week's worth of work. Days at the very least. What the heck?
text: 我们并没有一条很直接的路径把它做成一个很棒的版本。90 分钟处理 100 个 issue。我坐在那里回想，这本来得是一整周的工作量，至少也得好几天。搞什么啊？

speaker: Guest
text_en: And even more than that, Claude's analysis of at least half the issues pertained to things I knew nothing about. Where it was undeniably a smarter, better reviewer, programmer that I could ever dream to be. Well, not dream to be.
text: 更夸张的是，Claude 对至少一半 issue 的分析，涉及的东西我根本一无所知。在那些地方，它毫无疑问是个比我能想象到的还要更聪明、更厉害的 reviewer 和程序员。嗯，也不是说我梦想成为那样。

speaker: Host
text_en: But was in that moment. No, but you would have not put in the effort for those areas. This was why the PR sat in the first place in many cases. Because you knew that.
text: 但当时确实是这样。不是，不过那些方面你本来就不会投入精力。很多情况下 PR 一开始就搁在那里，就是因为这个。因为你心里知道。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I would look at it and go like, I think there's something here, but then I now have to read up on this Debus thing. I have to figure out, is this the right way of doing it? I don't want to just merge something that then has other issues.
text: 我会看一眼，然后想，这里面可能有点东西，但接下来我还得去补这个 Debus 的背景。我得搞清楚，这是不是正确的做法？我不想随手一 merge，结果又带出别的问题。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And to be able to do that, agent accelerated was one of top 20 programming moments. I like how you put agent accelerated.
text: 而要做到这一点，agent accelerated 算是我心目中编程领域前 20 的高光时刻之一。我喜欢你这个说法，agent accelerated。

speaker: Host
text_en: And it sounds like it's especially efficient for work that is waiting on you, but you don't want to do it or you're not as skilled at doing it, but it's a hassle to delegate. Because again, you have a team, right?
text: 而且听起来，它对那种卡在你这里的工作尤其高效：你不想做，或者你也没那么擅长做，但真要委派出去又很麻烦。毕竟你是有团队的，对吧？

speaker: Host
text_en: But you probably didn't delegate it because you probably knew that it wouldn't make it faster or better.
text: 但你大概率没委派，因为你心里也知道，那样既不会更快，也不会更好。

speaker: Host
text_en: So I wonder if there's a part of AI that, because we talk a lot about, like, you know, like companies love to measure, especially larger ones, like efficiency, PRs, and they want to see impact.
text: 所以我在想，AI 是不是有这样一部分价值：因为我们老在说，你懂的，公司很喜欢衡量，尤其是大公司，喜欢看效率、PR 数量，还想看到影响。

speaker: Guest
text_en: But about the impact of doing work that we would have not done before. That's the kicker for me. That's the fact that the pie is just exploding right now. It's not growing. It's exploding.
text: 但更关键的是，去看那些以前我们根本不会去做的工作所带来的影响。对我来说这才是重点。这意味着那个蛋糕现在是在爆炸式扩张，不是普通增长，而是在爆炸。

speaker: Guest
text_en: The number of projects we have tackled internally that we would never even have contemplated starting on a legion. We had a great project where normally on performance work, you worry about P50, P95, P99.
text: 我们内部已经推进了很多项目，过去我们连开始都不会考虑，数量多得惊人。我们有个很棒的项目，通常做性能优化时，你会关注 P50、P95、P99。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Jeremy, one of our most agent accelerated people went like, What about P1? What about the floor? Can we fix the floor? What is the floor? And he went like, Well, right now our floor is, I forget what it was, four milliseconds. Let's say that, right?
text: Jeremy，我们团队里一个最会用 agent 提速的人就说，P1 怎么办？性能下限呢？我们能不能把下限也优化掉？下限到底是多少？然后他说，嗯，我们现在的下限是——我忘了具体是多少了——4 毫秒吧，先这么说，对吧？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Well, actually, four milliseconds can add up if you have a bunch of fast requests, they can still, it still matters. And he just went like, we're gonna do P1. We're gonna optimize P1, literally the fastest 1% of requests.
text: 但其实，4 毫秒如果叠加在一堆很快的请求上，也还是会累积起来，还是有意义的。然后他就说，我们要做 P1。我们要优化 P1，也就是字面意义上最快的那 1% 请求。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We're gonna make them even faster. He took it from, I think it was four milliseconds, to less than half a millisecond. He 10xed the performance there. I was like, I would never have signed up on this.
text: 我们要让它们变得更快。他把它从我记得大概 4 毫秒，降到了不到 0.5 毫秒。那部分性能直接提升了 10 倍。我当时就想，这种项目以前我绝对不会批准。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And he did the P1 project over a couple of days as like a side gesheff. Because now he could. Now he could. Because he had a hunch. He had an intuition that there was something here. He let agents run with it.
text: 而且他把这个 P1 项目当成一个顺手做的副项目，花了几天就搞定了。因为现在他做得到。现在他真的做得到。因为他有个预感，有种直觉，觉得这里面有东西可挖。他就让 agents 放手去跑。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And the number of PRs that like, all right, we fixed this, we fixed this. I think total of the PR, the P1 project, maybe misremembering, but I think it was like 12 PRs.
text: 然后 PR 的数量也是，基本就是：好，这个修了，这个也修了。我记得整个 P1 项目加起来——也可能我记错了——大概有 12 个 PR。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Like just fixing all sorts of things where I look at the single PRs, I'm like, yeah, actually, okay, yeah, makes sense. I look at the total sum of it. You've changed 2,500 lines of code. You're like, you've done that in a few days.
text: 就是修了各种各样的问题。我看单个 PR 的时候，会觉得，嗯，确实，行，有道理。可等我看总量的时候，你已经改了 2500 行代码。而且你是几天内就做完的。

speaker: Host
text_en: And so I've never heard anyone do P1 because it just, it feels like a vanity project. Exactly. It makes no business sense. I mean, this is not true, right? Because everything adds up. But you know what I mean, right? I know exactly what you mean.
text: 所以我以前从没听过有人去做 P1，因为这听起来就像个面子工程。没错。它在商业上根本说不通。我的意思是，这么说也不完全对，对吧？因为所有东西都会累积。但你知道我的意思吧？我完全懂你的意思。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And this is exactly why the explosion of the pie suddenly lets us look at problems we would never have contemplated looking before. It's funny. I remember this scene from Terminator 2 where they found this chip from the Terminator in the first movie.
text: 而这也正是为什么，蛋糕一下子做大之后，我们突然可以去看那些以前根本不会考虑的问题。挺有意思的。我记得《Terminator 2》里有个场景，他们找到了第一部电影里那个 Terminator 留下来的芯片。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And he goes like, this thing gave us ideas we would never have investigated before. And like, there's some beautiful parallels here about like, maybe we're about to build the Terminator, the cliche. But also we're getting ideas.
text: 然后他就说，这东西给了我们一些以前根本不会去研究的想法。而且这里面有些很妙的对应关系，比如说，也许我们正要造出那个老套说法里的 Terminator。但与此同时，我们也在获得新点子。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We're getting ambitions. We would never have looked at before because suddenly the cost of exploring a hunch has just dropped by a thousand fold. I do this all the time now too.
text: 我们有了以前根本不会有的野心，因为现在探索一个直觉性想法的成本，一下子降了一千倍。我现在也老这么干。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I'll give it some vague, crappy instructions just because like I have this fleey idea. I haven't even crystallized it into a neat prompt. I just want to see something. It'll. And then I go like, oh, yeah. Delete. As in revert code back to normal.
text: 我会随手丢给它一些模糊、很烂的指令，因为我脑子里只有个飘忽的念头。我甚至都还没把它整理成一个像样的 prompt，我只是想先看看会出来什么。然后我就会说，哦，行。删掉。也就是把代码恢复正常。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I would be a little more precious about 75 lines of code because it would have taken me two hours to do them. Now there's no residual value to any of this stuff. And I could just go like, show me a draft.
text: 以前我会更舍不得那 75 行代码，因为我得花两个小时才能写出来。现在这些东西几乎没有什么残余价值了。我就可以直接说，给我看个草稿。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I feel a little bit like a king where you just go like, show me the analysis of the far-flung regions. Where are we with the tax?
text: 我会有点像个国王一样，张口就说，给我看看那些偏远地区的分析。税收那边现在什么情况？

speaker: Guest
text_en: And this boy is like, this servant is like, yes, I shall do so and will return in three weeks, except you can just wave your hands around and agents just come back with answers to stupid questions, terrible ideas.
text: 然后这个小伙子，这个仆人就会说，好的，我这就去办，三周后回来复命。只不过现在你只要挥挥手，各种 agent 就会带着答案回来，哪怕是那些蠢问题、烂点子。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Then suddenly, it wasn't so terrible. It was actually a great idea and you go like, wow, I did this with Umachi.
text: 结果突然之间，它也没那么烂了，反而成了个很棒的主意。你就会想，哇。我在 Umachi 上就这么干过。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I haven't even pulled the trigger on it yet, but one of the things with Umachi people have been asking for since the beginning is dual boot, being able to install Linux next to the Windows installation so that they can still play all their games.
text: 我甚至还没真的开始做这件事，不过 Umachi 有个功能是用户从一开始就在提的，就是双启动：能把 Linux 装在现有的 Windows 系统旁边，这样他们还能继续玩自己所有的游戏。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And I just went like, you know what? I have more than one computer, so when I play games, I can just do it on the PC. It's not a me problem. I totally get why a bunch of people want it. I'm not heavily inclined to spend four hours figuring it out.
text: 然后我当时就想，你知道吗？我又不止一台电脑，所以我要打游戏的话，直接在 PC 上玩就行了。这对我来说不算什么问题。我完全能理解为什么很多人想要这个。我自己并没有强烈到愿意花四个小时去折腾明白。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And I just, a little while ago, went like, oh, this is exactly the kind of problem. Like, I don't have to figure it out. Just make the agent figure it out. So I kicked off initially the process of just coming up with a plan.
text: 然后就在不久前，我突然觉得，哦，这不就是最适合这种问题的场景吗？就是我根本不用自己去搞明白，直接让 agent 去搞明白就行。所以我一开始先让它走一遍流程，先拿出个方案。

speaker: Guest
text_en: This is a pretty big change, right? Like if you fuck up someone's boot records or you overwrite their partition, the criticality high, which is one of the reasons I didn't want to engage with it.
text: 这其实是个挺大的改动，对吧？比如你要是把别人的启动记录搞坏了，或者把分区给覆盖了，那严重性就非常高，这也是我之前一直不想碰它的原因之一。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Secondly, it's a little finicky if you want Lux encryption on the Linux partition, but the Linux partition doesn't own the whole drive. It's a little hairy. I didn't want to take on the criticality.
text: 其次，如果你想在 Linux 分区上做 LUKS 加密，但那个 Linux 分区又不是独占整块硬盘，这事就会有点麻烦，挺棘手的。我不想承担这么高的风险。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I'm like, this is perfect for the kind of agent stuff. So it started off basically just having Opus and Codex ping pong a plan. Like, I'll just, I asked Opus first, like come up with a plan for this.
text: 我当时就想，这简直太适合 agent 这类事情了。所以一开始基本上就是让 Opus 和 Codex 来回 ping pong 一个方案。就是我先问 Opus，给这件事做个方案出来。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It thinks for minutes and minutes and then come up with a good plan. And then I kick it over to Codex and like critique the plan. And then I had him ping pong back and forth a couple of times.
text: 它会想上好几分钟，然后给出一个不错的方案。接着我再把方案丢给 Codex，让它去挑毛病、做点评。然后我又让它们来回折腾了几个回合。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And at the end looking at the plan going like, yup, that's a good plan. We should totally do that. And I can't wait to kick that one off and just go, yeah, now Omachi does dual boot. Not because I did it, but thank your helpful clinkers.
text: 最后我看着那个方案就觉得，嗯，对，这方案很好，完全可以这么干。我已经迫不及待想把它跑起来了，然后直接说，行，现在 Omachi 已经是双系统启动了。不是因为我自己做的，而是多亏了你那些热心的小 clinkers。

speaker: Host
text_en: That level of ambition is still something I've yet to internalize. Like even just that, that like, hey, here are these hunches or demands, projects that I would like to do and maybe someday. And you could kick it up on a hunch while you go to lunch.
text: 这种级别的野心，我到现在还是没完全建立起这种思维。哪怕只是那种感觉：嘿，我这儿有一些直觉、一些需求、一些我想做也许以后再做的项目。而你现在甚至可以凭一个念头就把它发出去，自己去吃个午饭。

speaker: Host
text_en: That is a new world, which is also one of the reasons I think a lot of people are thinking, well, the model continues to improve. But even if we somehow hit a wall tomorrow, the bitter lesson is no longer true. There's actually a limit.
text: 那是一个全新的世界，这也是为什么我觉得很多人在想：好吧，模型还会继续进步。但就算我们明天 somehow 真撞上了墙，“苦涩的教训”也不再成立了。实际上是有上限的。

speaker: Host
text_en: It's 19 trillion tokens. That's how much they can learn. Not true at all. But if it was and we had to be stuck with these models, we would spend the next decade just getting more and more out of them, learning how to use these tools.
text: 比如说上限是 19 万亿个 token，这就是它们能学到的全部。其实完全不是这么回事。但假如真是这样，而且我们只能停留在这些模型上，那接下来十年我们也会只是不断从它们身上榨出更多价值，学习怎么使用这些工具。

speaker: Host
text_en: You see this actually with vintage computers. So the kind of games they were able to make on the Commodore 64 when that was released back in 81 to 85, I think was the main run.
text: 其实你在老式电脑上也能看到这种情况。像 Commodore 64，1981 年发布之后到 1985 年左右，我觉得算是它的主要生命周期，那时候他们能做出来的游戏就是一种水平。

speaker: Host
text_en: I know they made it a little longer, but then the Amiga and other machines came out, were great games. I mean, I got interested in games because of the Commodore 64, year kung fu and all that stuff.
text: 我知道后来它其实又延续了一阵子，但之后 Amiga 和其他机器出来了，也有很多很棒的游戏。我的意思是，我当年就是因为 Commodore 64 才对游戏产生兴趣的，像 Year Kung-Fu 之类的那些。

speaker: Host
text_en: The stuff they were able to do 20 years later when someone had just noodled all the secrets and tweaked the one megahertz processor. When they're building games for the old, old products. Yes.
text: 但 20 年后，人们把各种门道都摸透了，把那颗 1 MHz 处理器调到极致时，他们能做出来的东西就完全不一样了。就是那种还在给这些非常非常老的产品开发游戏的时候。对。

speaker: Host
text_en: Are so much more technically impressive because we just know so much more about the, I mean, same thing with the, you look at the PlayStation, first games come out on launch, last games before we go to PlayStation 2, they look like from different generations.
text: 技术上会强太多，因为我们对这些东西的理解已经深得多了。我的意思是，PlayStation 也是一样，你看首发时出的第一批游戏，再看切到 PlayStation 2 之前的最后一批游戏，简直像是两个世代的产品。

speaker: Host
text_en: We could totally continue to do that with the models, but we're not going to have that particular enjoyment because there's a new model dropping in three months.
text: 我们完全也可以继续这样去挖掘这些模型，但我们不会有那种特定的乐趣，因为三个月后又会有一个新模型发布。

speaker: Host
text_en: But this is interesting because if we just run with this thought, like, of course we know new things are going to come, but the point is like, we will be spending so much time learning, applying them, building either our internal systems, changing how we build things, taking on new projects, like if you're an existing team.
text: 但这点很有意思，因为如果顺着这个思路往下想——当然我们知道新东西还会不断出现——关键在于，我们会花大量时间去学习它们、应用它们、搭建自己的内部系统、改变我们做事和构建产品的方式、接新的项目，尤其如果你本来就是一个现有团队的话。

speaker: Host
text_en: Now that people can do more work and more ambitious work, how are you thinking of the team taking on more work, launching more products? Are you thinking of, of potentially growing the team or keeping it as is?
text: 现在大家能做更多工作、也能做更有野心的工作了，你怎么看团队去承担更多工作、上线更多产品这件事？你会考虑把团队扩大，还是保持现在这样？

speaker: Guest
text_en: My best assessment for our setup is that the same people can do much more. Let's internalize that, but that's also enough. Already we were doing enough. Already we had margin that we could hire way more if we had enough good ideas for that.
text: 以我们现在这套配置来看，我最好的判断是，同样一批人可以做更多事。这个我们得先真正消化接受，但其实这也已经够了。我们本来就已经做得够多了。而且我们本来就有余量，只要有足够多的好点子，我们其实还能再招很多人。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So all this extra productivity we're getting out of the team allows us now to do things like P1 and these other projects that are awesome. And they're going to improve the product faster too. Of course they are.
text: 所以，团队现在多出来的这些生产力，让我们能去做像 P1 以及其他这些很棒的项目。而且它们也会更快地改进产品。当然会。

speaker: Guest
text_en: The old way of thinking, like it's going to take two months to deliver a major feature. I mean, that's out the door. Of course, there's going to be rapid acceleration. That's going to filter all the way into our software methodology process.
text: 以前那种思路，比如说交付一个重要功能要花两个月，我是说，那一套已经过时了。显然，速度会大幅提升，而且这种提升会一路渗透到我们的软件方法论和流程里。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Like ShapeUp was built on two month cycles. That doesn't make sense in the same way at all anymore. We have not fully rewritten those scripts yet because the acceleration is still so fast. No company really has rewritten the scripts on all of that.
text: 比如 ShapeUp 就是建立在两个月周期上的。现在那种做法已经完全不像以前那样说得通了。我们还没有把那套打法彻底重写，因为加速还在飞快发生。实际上，也没有哪家公司真的把这一整套东西都重写完。

speaker: Host
text_en: When you're shipping that much faster, you needed a way to control what goes live and measure whether it's working. This is a good time to mention our presenting sponsor, StatSig. Experimentation feature flags for teams. That ship fast.
text: 当你的发布速度快了这么多，你就需要一种方式来控制哪些内容会上线，并衡量它是否真的有效。这正好也适合提一下我们的首席赞助商 StatSig。为团队提供实验和功能开关，帮助你快速发布。

speaker: Host
text_en: StatSig build a unified platform that enables both experimentation and continuous shipping.
text: StatSig 打造了一个统一平台，同时支持实验和持续发布。

speaker: Host
text_en: Built-in experimentation means that every rollout automatically becomes a learning opportunity with proper statistical analysis showing you exactly how features impact your metrics. Feature flags let you ship continuously with confidence.
text: 内置实验功能意味着，每一次发布都会自动变成一次学习机会，并通过完善的统计分析，准确告诉你各项功能会怎样影响你的指标。功能开关则让你能够更有把握地持续发布。

speaker: Host
text_en: And because it's all in one platform with the same product data, teams across your organization can collaborate and make data-driven decisions. com slash pragmatic. With this, let's get back to the shift about to hit developers.
text: 而且因为这一切都在同一个平台上、用的是同一套产品数据，你们组织里的各个团队就能协作起来，做出数据驱动的决策。com slash pragmatic。好，说到这儿，我们回到那个即将冲击开发者的转变。

speaker: Guest
text_en: But I still think software developers are delusional if they do not think a shift is coming where before they were the constraint on how much could be produced and therefore could command the salaries that flow to the constraints.
text: 但我还是觉得，如果软件开发者不认为一场转变正在到来，那就是在自欺欺人。以前他们是产能的瓶颈，所以也就能拿到那些会流向瓶颈岗位的薪资。

speaker: Guest
text_en: If suddenly those constraints now loosen, especially if we fast forward a little bit where the product manager is actually able to produce changes that can be shipped and work, things are going to change.
text: 如果这些瓶颈突然开始松动，尤其是我们把时间稍微往后拨一点，到了产品经理实际上都能做出可以上线、而且能正常工作的改动的时候，事情就会变了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I do actually think if I was going to bet, we've seen peak programmer.
text: 如果要我下注的话，我觉得我们已经见到了程序员的巅峰时刻。

speaker: Guest
text_en: In terms of the learned guild of programmers who went to either school or spent umpteen hours getting really good at it, we're not going to need the same number of them to do the same amount of work.
text: 就那些受过训练的程序员群体来说，不管是科班出身，还是花了不知道多少个小时把这件事练到非常熟，我们以后不再需要同样数量的人去完成同样规模的工作了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Now, Javon's paradox, where as the price of something goes down, you get more of it or you get more demand for it. It's true, but that doesn't mean that all programmers are going to get bailed out by it.
text: 当然，Javon's paradox 说的是，当某样东西的价格下降时，你会得到更多它，或者说对它的需求会增加。这没错，但这也不意味着所有程序员都会因此被救回来。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Just because more software than ever is going to be produced. That's for sure. By the way, I think GitHub has gotten a lot of slack or flack lately. Justifiably so. I saw a chart saying they had a 92% uptime, which sounds insane.
text: 仅仅因为未来产出的软件会比以往任何时候都更多——这点是肯定的。顺便说一句，我觉得 GitHub 最近挨了不少批评，而且也算是有道理。我看到一张图说他们的 uptime 只有 92%，这听起来简直离谱。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I'm not sure exactly what that was measuring, but I feel it. I have a little bit of sympathy in that. I also think there's some mistakes were made, but also that the amount of software that's currently being produced is on a rocket ship.
text: 我不太确定那具体是在衡量什么，但我能感受到那种情况。对此我多少有点同情。我也觉得确实犯过一些错误，不过与此同时，现在软件产出的规模也正在像坐火箭一样往上冲。

speaker: Host
text_en: We are producing as a civilization globally way more software than we've ever done before. I mean, OpenClaw itself, I thought he said it was 400,000 lines of code. That used to take 10 years and 2000 people to get to that.
text: 从全球整个文明的尺度来看，我们现在产出的软件，比以往任何时候都多得多。就说 OpenClaw 本身吧，我记得他说是 40 万行代码。以前做到这个规模，得花 10 年、要 2000 个人。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Well, not 2000, but yes, it took a lot. I mean, a long time, right? You look at, I think, the main monolith at Shopify is 3 million lines of code. That's 20 years.
text: 嗯，也不是 2000 人，但对，确实得投入很多。我的意思是，要花很长时间，对吧？你看，我记得 Shopify 那个主要的单体应用大概有 300 万行代码。那是 20 年积累出来的。

speaker: Host
text_en: And if you collectively sum up all programmers who've worked on that, probably like 20,000 people. Yeah, big shifts are coming right now. Lots of software is being produced.
text: 如果把所有参与过那个项目的程序员都加起来，可能得有 2 万人。对，现在大的变化正在发生。软件产出非常多。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I can see why it's creaking a little bit over there because the pushes are just going to accelerate, right? And we haven't even seen anything yet. If you look at AI adoption curves, basically no one's using it.
text: 所以我能理解为什么那边现在已经有点吃不消了，因为这种推进速度只会越来越快，对吧？而且我们其实还什么都没真正看到呢。如果你看 AI 的采用曲线，基本上几乎没人真正在用。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We all in our little bubble in X are like, ah, everyone's able. No, they're not. Most companies in the world are just not doing it. Notwithstanding that, I think ChatGPT got to 800 million users very quickly.
text: 我们这些在 X 这个小圈子里的人会觉得，啊，大家都会用了。不是的，根本不是。世界上大多数公司其实都还没在做这个。即便如此，我觉得 ChatGPT 还是非常快地达到了 8 亿用户。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Obviously, there was adoption, but nothing on the scale of what the companies that are furthest along are doing and how much they're accelerating with it.
text: 显然，采用是有的，但规模远远比不上那些走在最前面的公司正在做的事情，也比不上它给他们带来的加速幅度。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So I do think it is correct for the average programmer to think maybe we've seen the best of the golden days.
text: 所以我确实觉得，普通程序员如果认为黄金时代最好的日子可能已经过去了，这种想法是有道理的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Certainly there will be pressures on price because one thing are companies like ours that have essentially unlimited scope to come up with new features and do more. And we can then plow in all that additional productivity into just do more.
text: 当然，价格上肯定会有压力，因为一类是像我们这样的公司，基本上有近乎无限的空间去想新功能、做更多事情。然后我们就可以把这些额外提升出来的生产力，全部继续投入到做更多事上。

speaker: Guest
text_en: There's also a lot of companies who just need to do a thing. And if they can do that thing at a tenth of the cost, that's actually their advantage, right? They just need to do this thing. It's very neatly scoped and defined. It's a cost center.
text: 还有很多公司其实只是需要把某件事做出来。如果他们能用原来十分之一的成本把这件事做成，那反而就是他们的优势，对吧？他们只需要把这件事做好。范围非常清晰，定义也很明确。它就是个成本中心。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Anywhere where software development is a cost center, which is actually probably the majority of software development in the world. They're going to face these pressures. Yeah.
text: 凡是软件开发被当作成本中心的地方——而这其实很可能是全世界大多数软件开发的现实——都会面临这种压力。对。

speaker: Host
text_en: Sounds like if I'm a software engineer right now and I'm worried about like, well, you know, I just want to make sure that I'm I'm at a place where things are going to be better.
text: 听起来像是，如果我现在是一名软件工程师，而且我在担心，嗯，你知道，我只是想确保自己待的地方未来会越来越好。

speaker: Guest
text_en: You want to be at a place where you want to either get out of a cost center or become really valuable there. Obviously, you know, brush up your skills.
text: 你要去那种地方：要么能脱离成本中心，要么能在里面变得非常有价值。显然，你也得提升一下自己的技能。

speaker: Host
text_en: And also I'm wondering if if the shape of software engineers who will be hired will be changing because if, if I just look back from like the nineties, right, like even if you look at the movies, you saw the stereotypes.
text: 我还在想，未来会被招聘的软件工程师画像是不是也会变化。因为如果我回头看，比如从九十年代开始，对吧，哪怕看电影，你都能看到那种刻板印象。

speaker: Host
text_en: They were the nerds who didn't talk to anyone, but they knew how to code. They knew how to do assembly. And then we went in the two thousands, it was still based on languages.
text: 他们就是那种不跟人说话的书呆子，但他们会写代码，懂汇编。到了两千年代，招聘标准也还是以编程语言为基础。

speaker: Host
text_en: And over time, I think in the 2010s, startups started to not hire for languages, but just hire for algorithms because you could learn the stuff.
text: 再往后，我觉得到了 2010 年代，创业公司开始不再按语言招人了，而是按算法能力招，因为那些具体的东西你是可以学的。

speaker: Host
text_en: And now I'm seeing companies, some of the, the latest VC funded companies hire for product engineers where they, they're actually asking for like empathy, communication on top of, like, it's kind of a given that you, you know how to code or whatever.
text: 而现在我看到一些最新拿到 VC 投资的公司，在招的是产品工程师。他们实际上会要求同理心、沟通能力这些东西，除此之外，像会写代码之类的，反而已经默认你具备了。

speaker: Host
text_en: So I wonder if I'm just looking at just, just this curve, right? If I'm just painting it up, like you're starting to get people, oh, and, and the developers that I meet at all these companies, they're all really pleasant. They're all.
text: 所以我在想，是不是我只是看到了这条曲线的一部分，对吧？如果我只是把它往好的方向去描，那你就会开始发现，哦，而且我在这些公司里遇到的开发者，人都特别好，都很——

speaker: Guest
text_en: Which is very communicative, very, oh, and they talk with customers, most of them. Just, it's not even a drag. It's like, and more and more of them love doing it. That's the constraint value now.
text: 而且都特别善于沟通，特别——哦，他们大多数还会直接跟客户交流。这甚至都不算负担。更像是，而且越来越多人还很喜欢做这件事。现在真正的约束价值就在这里。

speaker: Guest
text_en: The constraint value is figuring out what should we build, how should it be built, which customers should we be talking to, where should we be focusing? It's product management.
text: 现在的约束价值在于搞清楚：我们该做什么，应该怎么做，应该跟哪些客户聊，重点该放在哪儿。这其实就是产品管理。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It's so funny for me too, because historically I've not necessarily had the highest esteem for product management as a function. I thought there was a lot of bullshit, and I thought it was a lot of people who maybe didn't do as much, right?
text: 这对我来说也挺好笑的，因为历史上我未必一直很看得起产品管理这个职能。我觉得里面有很多扯淡的东西，也觉得有不少人可能并没有做那么多事，对吧？

speaker: Guest
text_en: And one of the reasons was that they couldn't because the constraint resource was the implementation, was the product manager could find out that they wanted to do something.
text: 其中一个原因就是，他们其实也做不了太多，因为真正受约束的资源是落地实现。也就是说，产品经理可以搞清楚他们想做某件事。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I want to do this feature and then they had to wait four weeks for some very expensive programmers to make that reality happen. And in those four weeks, I mean, I guess they could go talk to some. They were unutilized.
text: 比如说，我想做这个功能，然后他们就得等四周，等一些成本很高的程序员把这件事真正做出来。而在这四周里，我是说，他们大概也只能再去找一些人聊聊。他们其实没有被充分利用。

speaker: Guest
text_en: They were not the constraint, right? The constraint was on the implementation. That absolutely is going to switch. And now pure implementation is going to be solved at some point.
text: 他们不是那个约束点，对吧？约束点在实现端。这绝对会发生转变。到某个时候，纯粹的实现问题会被解决掉。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I'm not claiming it is right now and anyone who is, have not tried to just deploy five coded stuff with no review to major code bases.
text: 我不是说现在已经这样了，任何这么说的人，肯定都没试过在没有评审的情况下，直接把五份 AI 写出来的代码部署到大型代码库里。

speaker: Guest
text_en: But as the lesson of last summer on Lex, I'm not going to put my heart on the block and saying that's not going to happen before next summer.
text: 但吸取了去年夏天在 Lex 那件事的教训，我可不会把话说死，不会拍着胸口说这种事在明年夏天之前绝对不会发生。

speaker: Host
text_en: Again, this is just like common sense, but implementation, one implementation, what we saw for, for a general use case for the edge cases, it will take longer. And for some cases, it will not make sense.
text: 再说一次，这其实就是常识，但真正落地时，某一种实现方式——我们看到的那种适用于一般场景的——到了边缘案例就会花更久。而且有些场景里，它根本就不合理。

speaker: Host
text_en: Same thing as I don't know, self-driving is fine for like these size of cars, but for like trucks, it'll either take longer or if you're specialized, but the point is like there will be pockets where, but those pockets will be smaller. Yes.
text: 就像，我也不知道，自动驾驶放在这种尺寸的车上可能没问题，但像卡车这种，要么会花更久，要么得做高度专门化。重点是，总会有一些这样的细分角落，不过这些角落会越来越小。对吧。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I do think the stereotype of, I just want to sit and code. You have to be John Carmack levels of good to retain that privilege to just, I just want to sit and code. And even John Carmack. And of course, he's also super AI appeal.
text: 我确实觉得那种“我就想坐着写代码”的刻板印象——你得强到 John Carmack 那个级别，才能保住这种特权，才能说“我就想坐着写代码”。甚至连 John Carmack 也是。而且当然了，他对 AI 也特别有吸引力。

speaker: Host
text_en: Well, but also like he, he also saw some trends that he could do. Like, for example, like, just like, you know, the type of games that people would buy, right? Like he needed to have some business skills or just surrounded by people who did that.
text: 不过他其实也看到了自己可以抓住的一些趋势。比如说，你知道的，玩家会买什么类型的游戏，对吧？所以他也得具备一些商业能力，或者至少身边得有懂这些的人。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Totally, totally, totally. But like you, you need to literally be the very best and not just the very best, but you need to be better than the agents, right?
text: 完全同意，完全同意，完全同意。但你真的得是最顶尖的，不只是最顶尖，你还得比那些 agent 更强，对吧？

speaker: Host
text_en: For you to get the privilege to just be an implementer, you have to be better than what's available off the shelf from, from agents. So who are the very best?
text: 你想拥有“只做执行的人”这种特权，你就必须比市面上现成可用的 agent 还要强。所以，谁才是最顶尖的那批人？

speaker: Host
text_en: And you're a good person to ask because whenever you advertise a position and this was even well before AI, I remember that you, you put out a job for a software engineer and a designer.
text: 这个问题问你很合适，因为每次你发布招聘岗位时——这甚至还是在 AI 出现之前——我记得你发过一个 software engineer 和 designer 的职位。

speaker: Host
text_en: And actually I want, I want to interview your designer who you hired and because you published the salary, which is a San Francisco salary. You put the exact number. You can check it for it.
text: 其实我还想采访一下你们招的那位设计师，因为你们把薪资公开了，而且那是 San Francisco 的薪资。你们把具体数字都写出来了，大家都能查到。

speaker: Host
text_en: You have a social media presence, so it kind of goes wide and you get a lot of applications and you do a pretty good job as, as I understand, you try to be very fair. You put a lot of effort into it. So what did it take to get hired at 37 signals?
text: 你们在社交媒体上也有影响力，所以传播得很广，会收到很多申请。而且据我了解，你们在招聘上做得相当不错，尽量做到非常公平，也投入了很多精力。所以，要想被 37 Signals 录用，到底需要什么？

speaker: Host
text_en: Because now you are trying to hire some of the best and based off of this, what advice do you give to people who are like, okay, I want to be the best in, in this age right now? Incredibly good question. No one has figured out, we haven't cracked it.
text: 因为你们现在是在招最优秀的一批人。基于这一点，你会给那些在想“好，我现在这个时代想成为最顶尖的人”的人什么建议？这个问题问得特别好。其实没人真正搞明白，我们也还没破解这个问题。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And I say that as someone who have run an organization where we, we must have looked at tens of thousands of... Now, of course, if you're running Google, you've looked at millions, but we've looked at tens of thousands of candidates.
text: 我这么说，是因为我自己就管理过一个组织，我们肯定看过成千上万份……当然，如果你是在 Google，那你看过的可能是几百万份，但我们也看过成千上万名候选人。

speaker: Guest
text_en: The number of candidates we've hired is quite small. I mean, total number of programmers that have been through 37 Signals over its entire lifespan. What's that gonna be like, I don't know, a hundred, 150 at the most. I haven't even done the math.
text: 但我们最终录用的候选人数量其实很少。我的意思是，37 Signals 整个生命周期里来来去去的程序员总数有多少？大概也就，我不知道，最多一百到一百五十人吧。我甚至都还没认真算过。

speaker: Host
text_en: How big is your team right now-ish? We're 60 people at the entire company and we are, what's that gonna be like, 20 programmers, something like that? Yeah, that's probably about right. And so, so what's the other 40 folks?
text: 你们现在团队大概有多大？我们整个公司有 60 个人，然后我们有，大概多少来着，20 个程序员，差不多吧？对，应该差不多。所以，另外那 40 个人都是做什么的？

speaker: Guest
text_en: We have designers, probably like 10 of those. Wow. And then we have customer support, which is at 14. Then we have a bunch of support functions, HR, finance, and then we have operations. Operations is quite large.
text: 我们有设计师，大概 10 个。哇。然后客户支持团队有 14 个人。再就是一些支持职能，HR、财务，然后还有运维。运维团队规模挺大的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We have 10 folks managing all our servers and yeah, that's about it. But yeah, I probably, it's probably about a hundred people in total that I've worked with or employed at the company as programmers out of... Tens of thousands. Yeah.
text: 我们有 10 个人在管理所有服务器，差不多就是这些了。不过是的，我合作过或者在公司里雇过的程序员，总共大概也就 100 人左右，而候选人却是……成千上万。对。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We've looked at. And even all those hires did not pan out in the long term. Like I'd actually say, I think I looked at this recently. Our batting average at best, I think slightly better than 50/50. So half of even those hires...
text: 我们也研究过这个。甚至那些招进来的人，从长期看也并没有都成。其实我最近还看了下，我觉得我们的命中率最好也就略高于五五开。所以就连那些 hire 进来的人里，也有一半……

speaker: Host
text_en: Half go through all of it because you have a really long and thorough process. Yes. You put a lot of effort, right? No one has figured out just to hire with such efficiency that they don't make mistakes.
text: 有一半人经历了那么长、那么彻底的流程之后还是不行。对。你们投入了很多精力，对吧？到现在也没人能把招聘做到高效到完全不犯错。

speaker: Guest
text_en: There's a great paper that Google published quite a long time ago now, where they tried all sorts of different hypotheses. Well, can we predict employee outcomes on the basis of Ivy League education background, on GPA, on... All of these things.
text: Google 很早以前发过一篇很棒的论文，他们测试了各种不同的假设。比如说，我们能不能根据员工是不是 Ivy League 出身、GPA、还有……这些因素来预测员工后续表现。

speaker: Guest
text_en: The conclusion was basically like, we know nothing. We can't predict it on any of these things. We can't predict it on LeetCode. We can't predict it on any of these metrics.
text: 结论基本就是：我们其实什么都不知道。靠这些东西根本预测不了。靠 LeetCode 预测不了，靠任何这类指标都预测不了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Now, what I'd say is I've clearly been spoiled by working with some very good people, not just at my company, but in open source in general.
text: 不过我要说的是，我显然是被一群非常优秀的人给惯坏了，不只是我公司里的同事，也包括整个开源圈子里的人。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And therefore, I've ended up with occasionally a twisted perspective of what the average programmer is capable of. And when we do hiring rounds, I am sometimes, well, not sometimes.
text: 所以结果就是，我有时候会对普通程序员平均能做到什么，形成一种有点偏掉的认知。等到我们进入招聘轮次的时候，我有时，呃，也不只是有时。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Every time, I'm kind of surprised how poor the majority of the submissions are, how little effort is put into being presentable. And that can sound really boomer crotchety very quickly. But it's also just the reality of trying to get a job.
text: 其实每次我都会有点惊讶，大多数提交的东西质量有多差，为了让自己看起来像样一点，投入的精力又有多少。这话听起来很容易像那种老派爱抱怨的人在唠叨。但这也是找工作的现实。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Like, you got to stand out. And I understand that that's uncomfortable, right? Like, who wants to look at this as like, well, the odds are kind of against me. But it's also a trap to actually fall into thinking of this in terms of odds.
text: 你就是得让自己脱颖而出。我也理解这会让人不舒服，对吧？谁会愿意用“反正概率对我不利”这种方式来看这件事呢？但真把它当成概率问题去想，其实也是个陷阱。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Because what I've seen, the miscalculation happen time and again, is people go like, okay, so you have a thousand applicants. There's only one who gets the job or maybe two who gets the job. 1% chance. No, it's not. Not at all.
text: 因为我看到过一次又一次的误判是，人们会觉得，好，你有一千个申请者，最后只有一个人拿到工作，或者也许两个人拿到工作，那就是 1% 的概率。不是，根本不是这么回事。

speaker: Guest
text_en: With that math, you had 0% chance. Zero. And the very best, they probably had a 10% chance, 20%, 30% chance. It is not equal distributed. It is not a lottery.
text: 按这种算法，你的机会其实是 0%。零。而最优秀的人，他们大概有 10%、20%、30% 的机会。机会根本不是平均分布的，这不是抽彩票。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We don't just, like, pick a thing out and be like, oh, it's going to be this person because they happen to be the one drawn from the bunch. Not at all. We discard off the bat probably at least half the applications. Maybe it's two-thirds.
text: 我们不是随便抓一个出来，然后说，哦，就这个人吧，因为他刚好从这一堆人里被抽中了。完全不是。我们一开始就会刷掉至少一半的申请，可能甚至是三分之二。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Just because they're either not addressing the job directly, they are not following the instructions in the relatively clear spoken written openings that we have, right? They're obviously not right for it or whatever, or we get some other smells.
text: 原因很简单，要么他们的申请根本没有直接回应这个岗位，要么他们没有按照我们那些其实已经写得相当清楚的招聘说明来做，对吧？要么明显就不合适，或者我们闻到了别的苗头。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Then there's, like, perhaps a third left. And then we start looking at some of the submissions. Then we narrow it down historically to a pool of around 20 people that we give an at-home test. The at-home test is wonderful. Some people hate it.
text: 然后大概会剩下三分之一。接着我们开始认真看这些提交材料。历史上我们通常会把范围缩小到大约 20 个人，给他们一个 take-home test。这个 take-home test 很棒，虽然有些人很讨厌它。

speaker: Guest
text_en: They feel like it's free labor. I'm like, what the fuck are you talking about? I'm not going to use your submission to a code test. I'm going to deploy it to production. How do you think we came up with that code test?
text: 他们觉得那是在白嫖劳动力。我就想说，你他妈在说什么？我又不会把你 code test 的提交拿去直接部署到生产环境。你觉得我们这个 code test 是怎么想出来的？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Because it already exists in the system. I say that a little harshly. I also get the sympathy of, like, I don't want to put six hours into making a test if it's not going anywhere. Okay, I get it, but there's no way around it.
text: 因为它本来就在系统里。我这话说得有点重了。但我也能理解那种心情，就是我不想花六个小时做个测试，结果最后一点下文都没有。好，我懂，但这事没法绕开。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Because if you have it in your head that you just send in a resume, someone's going to call you up on the phone, have a 30-minute conversation with you, and go, you've hired, sir!
text: 因为如果你脑子里想的是，我只要投一份简历，就会有人打电话给我，跟我聊 30 分钟，然后说，先生，你被录用了！那可不是这么运作的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I don't know if that ever existed, but certainly it does not exist today. It never existed in the lifetime that I've been in this business. Well, the only time it exists, right, is through a very warm referral where you're starting a company.
text: 我不知道那种情况以前是否真的存在过，但至少今天肯定不存在了。在我从业的这些年里，它从来就没存在过。嗯，唯一会出现这种情况的时候，就是你通过非常熟的人脉推荐去创业。

speaker: Guest
text_en: If you're skipping the whole pipeline. And when you skip the whole pipeline, typically it only happens at the very beginning of a company, when you're founding a company.
text: 也就是你直接跳过了整套招聘流程。而跳过整套流程，通常只会发生在公司最早期、也就是创办公司的时候。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And often it goes both ways where it's very risky, and then you say, like, this buddy of mine, I worked with this person for two years straight. I would, like, trust them with my eyes closed.
text: 而且很多时候这是双向的，因为风险很高，所以你会说，比如，这是我哥们儿，我跟这个人一起密切共事了整整两年。我基本上可以闭着眼睛信任他。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So that's actually the black pill on the whole hiring process. If we look at the long-term success rates, we have had more long-term employees from, I've worked with this person for two years, we should hire them, than we have from the open calls.
text: 所以这其实就是整个招聘流程里那个很残酷的真相。如果看长期成功率的话，那种“我跟这个人共事过两年，我们应该招他”的情况，最后留下来的长期员工，比公开招聘渠道招来的还要多。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It is actually exceptionally difficult. It has been for us to find the kind of programmer who thrives in our environment from open call. It has happened. We have hired people that way.
text: 实际上，这真的特别难。对我们来说，想通过公开招聘找到那种能在我们这种环境里如鱼得水的程序员，异常困难。当然，也不是没发生过。我们确实也这样招到过人。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And I continue to want to believe, even if the odds seem insanely long when you start doing the math of, like, oh, my God, we've looked at tens of thousands and how many of them got hired and how many of them didn't work out.
text: 而且我还是愿意继续相信这条路，哪怕你一开始认真算这个账时，会发现概率低得离谱：天啊，我们看了成千上万份，有多少人被录用了，又有多少人最后并不合适。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Like, Jesus, there's only, like, a handful left. I'm starting that. That's kind of blackmailing. But then hiring directly on the base of a warm referral, as you call it, has worked very well. And that, the hit rate there is really high.
text: 天哪，最后真正留下来的也就那么几个人。我刚才那么说，听起来都有点让人绝望了。但像你说的那种基于熟人强力推荐直接招人，效果一直都非常好。那种方式的命中率真的很高。

speaker: Guest
text_en: But how does that help anyone? Right? Like, that's not a very actionable advice, except that's to say, get as good as you can get and put in as much effort as you can and work with someone. Because I want to say that that does account.
text: 但这对别人有什么帮助呢，对吧？这其实并不是特别可执行的建议。除非你把它理解成：尽可能把自己练得更强，尽可能多投入，并且去和别人一起共事。因为我想说，这一点确实很重要。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Some people have this notion in their head that if they work at a place they consider shitty, they shouldn't try. You're shooting your own feet, buddy.
text: 有些人脑子里有个想法，觉得如果自己在一个他们认为很烂的地方工作，就没必要认真。兄弟，你这是在搬起石头砸自己的脚。

speaker: Guest
text_en: If you show up at the shitty place of work, and we can even be objectively in unison about that, that it is a shitty place of work, and you then go, like, well, I should just try to skirt. I should just try to goof off.
text: 如果你去了一个很烂的工作环境上班，而且我们甚至都可以客观一致地承认，那地方就是很烂，然后你就想，嗯，那我干脆划划水算了，我就混一混吧。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I should just try to read X or Reddit all day. Right? Everyone else you work with, they're going to watch that. You know where that warm referral is going to come from?
text: 我就整天看 X 或 Reddit 就好了，对吧？跟你一起工作的其他人都会看在眼里。你知道那种靠谱的内推会从哪来吗？

speaker: Guest
text_en: It's going to come from someone who works with someone else at a shitty job but identified that that individual still showed up and did as best as they could to learn, to ship, to do all of this stuff. There is no shortcut here.
text: 它会来自某个在烂工作里和别人共事过的人，但他注意到那个人即便在那种环境里，还是照样认真上班，尽自己最大努力去学习、去交付、去把这些事做好。这里没有捷径。

speaker: Guest
text_en: You simply just have to be good. And you will not get good if you do not practice. And if you think your place of employment is not worthy of your best, you're cheating yourself.
text: 你就是得真的有本事。你不练，就不可能变强。如果你觉得你工作的地方不值得你拿出最好的状态，那你其实是在坑自己。

speaker: Guest
text_en: If you're not helping, even if it's a shitty place, if you're not helping that place get better, why would a great place hire you, who only hires people to further raise the bar? This is total cope.
text: 就算那地方很烂，如果你都不愿意帮它变得更好，那一个优秀的公司为什么要招你？人家招人本来就是为了把标准拉得更高。这完全是在自我安慰。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And it's cope both on the side of I work at a shitty place and therefore I don't want to put things in. You could be annoyed. I'm not telling you you have to love your boss.
text: 而且这种自我安慰两边都有：一边是“我在一个烂地方工作，所以我不想投入”。你当然可以不爽，我也不是说你非得喜欢你的老板。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I'd actually say the majority of people I used to work for, I didn't have the warmest feelings about them.
text: 说真的，我以前共事过的大多数上司，我对他们也谈不上多有好感。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I still tried really hard for my own edification, for my own education, for my own sense of I'm the kind of person who shows up and does a good job, just that I will be ready when the opportunity arrives, when all my talents are needed and all my skills are honed, right?
text: 我还是会非常努力，这既是为了自我提升、为了自己的学习，也是为了维护一种自我认同：我是那种会到场、会把事情做好的人。这样等机会来的时候，等到我所有才能都派上用场、所有技能都打磨成熟的时候，我就准备好了，对吧？

speaker: Host
text_en: Was this not how you ended up at 37signals, where it was just a contract job or something? And, you know, like on a contract job, you have no ownership and, but you showed up and correct.
text: 你后来去 37signals，不就是这么发生的吗？当时那好像就只是个合同工之类的工作吧？而且你知道，做合同工你是没有股权的，但你还是照样到场、把事做好。对吧。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And Jason ended up realizing, okay, this punk better get some equity. Otherwise he's out the door. Now that's a seminal story and you shouldn't extrapolate everything from that.
text: 然后 Jason 最后意识到，好吧，这小子最好得给点股权，不然他就要走人了。当然，这是个很关键的故事，但你也不该从这个故事里把所有事情都类推出来。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I mean, all founder stories, by the way, are seminal stories in that regard, but the fundamental principle is still the same. Show up, do as good as you can, learn more.
text: 我是说，顺便讲一句，所有创始人的故事在这方面某种程度上都很关键，但底层原则还是一样的：到场，尽你所能做到最好，学更多东西。

speaker: Guest
text_en: There also was, to my chagrin, to some extent, I perhaps contributed to it a bit for a while, which was this notion that you can be a great programmer and not really like programming, that you don't have to ever care about programming outside working hours.
text: 另外还有一种说法，说实话这让我有点懊恼，而且某种程度上我可能有一阵子也助长了这种想法。那就是：你可以成为一个很棒的程序员，却并不真的喜欢编程；你也完全没必要在工作时间之外还关心编程。

speaker: Host
text_en: Was this what you thought of or like Well, I thought of it mistakenly because I was pushing back on the overwork, hundred hour a week, 120 hour a week, maniacal obsession, which, by the way, never was my experience.
text: 这是你当时的想法吗？或者说——嗯，我当时是误以为是这样，因为我是在反对那种过度工作、每周干 100 小时、120 小时、近乎疯魔式的痴迷。顺便说一句，那从来都不是我的亲身经历。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We did not start Basecamp that way. We have worked on a 40 hour week rolling average over those 25 years. But also, as I said at the very beginning, I really like computers. So I play with computers in my free time.
text: 我们当初创办 Basecamp 不是那样起步的。过去这 25 年里，我们的滚动平均工作时长一直是每周 40 小时。但同时，就像我一开始说的，我真的很喜欢电脑，所以我在空闲时间也会玩电脑。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I look at computer things in my free time. It's not work in the sense that I'm whatever, shipping features to Basecamp customers, like just 24 7. That's not what it is. But I am playing with computers. I am looking at new things.
text: 我在空闲时间也会看各种和电脑相关的东西。这不是工作，不是说我在 24/7 地给 Basecamp 客户发功能、做交付，不是那个意思。但我确实会摆弄电脑，也会去看新东西。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I am exploring new systems and whatever. And I think there was for a while into the 2010s, a misconception that you didn't have to do any of those things.
text: 我也在探索新的系统之类的东西。我觉得在 2010 年代那阵子，有一种误解，认为你根本不需要做这些事。

speaker: Guest
text_en: You could just show up and do your work and you would be so sought after because programming was such a valuable activity and there were so few people who could do it that they'd take anyone, even people who barely gave a shit.
text: 你只要出现、把活干了，就会特别抢手，因为编程太有价值了，而会做的人又太少，所以他们谁都要，哪怕是那种根本不上心的人。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And I think that's over if it ever was true. And I think it was true. The boot camps were the perfect, like catalyst or like they were the canary when was What, by the way, is how the economy is supposed to function.
text: 我觉得这种时代已经结束了，如果它真的存在过的话。而我觉得它确实存在过。训练营就像是完美的催化剂，或者说像煤矿里的金丝雀，顺便说一句，这其实也是经济本来就该有的运作方式。

speaker: Guest
text_en: When salaries are really high, it means that there's not enough supply of labor. Therefore we should get labor into the pool. Exactly. And so I'm not, I don't even have any qualms about any of it. I'm just saying like that's over.
text: 当薪资特别高的时候，就说明劳动力供给不够。所以我们就该把更多劳动力引入这个池子里。没错。所以我对这整件事也没什么意见，我只是说，那种时代结束了。

speaker: Host
text_en: No, I think looking, you know, we're talking about like, is the golden age of the programmer? Have you passed peak programmer?
text: 对，我觉得回过头来看，我们其实是在说，程序员的黄金时代是不是已经过去了？是不是已经过了程序员的巅峰期？

speaker: Host
text_en: And I wonder if peak programmer really meant that almost anyone who wanted to get into the industry and was willing to put in some effort few months or maybe a few years could do it. You could learn how to code.
text: 我在想，所谓程序员的巅峰期，是不是其实意味着几乎任何想进这个行业、并且愿意花点时间努力的人，几个月或者也许几年，就都能做到。你可以学会写代码。

speaker: Host
text_en: You could go to either college or to a bootcamp or put in the hours and you could get hired at a place because the interviews were, the references were not needed. We didn't check. And that it's probably coming to an end. You do need references.
text: 你可以去上大学，或者去训练营，或者自己花时间钻研，然后就能在某个地方找到工作，因为那时候面试没那么严，也不需要背调。我们不会查。而现在这种情况可能快结束了。你确实需要推荐人了。

speaker: Host
text_en: You more, I think more and more companies will be doing reference checks as part of their thing. And it's not just going to be a heavy work there, like, would you, I've had these calls from like Databricks is famous for reference calls.
text: 而且我觉得会有越来越多公司把推荐人调查当成流程的一部分。不再只是那种随便走个过场，比如会问“你会不会……”，我就接过这种电话，像 Databricks 就是出了名会打推荐人电话的。

speaker: Host
text_en: They don't really check for references. They drill you, not just, would you work with this person again? What were their weaknesses? Right. Where would you hire them, et cetera, et cetera. I understand it.
text: 他们其实不会真的去做背景调查。他们会追着你问，不只是“你还愿意再和这个人共事吗？”还会问“他们的弱点是什么？”对吧。你会在哪种岗位上雇他们，诸如此类。我能理解。

speaker: Guest
text_en: The weird thing is peak programmer sounds like this is something that affects all programmers. It does not. The best programmers are not even the best, as in like, it's 10 people around the world.
text: 奇怪的是，“程序员巅峰”这个说法听起来像是会影响所有程序员，但其实并不是。最顶尖的程序员甚至都不是那种“全世界就十个人”级别的意思。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Really good programmers are currently more valuable than ever because they're the ones who are able to get the most out of the AI acceleration.
text: 现在，真正优秀的程序员比以往任何时候都更有价值，因为他们最能把 AI 带来的加速效应发挥出来。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And this was the kicker for me in changing my perspective on this, is that I've also found, and maybe it's not universally true, but certainly within 37signals, in my own experience, I'm enjoying my time as a programmer more than any time since early 2000s when I just discovered Ruby.
text: 而这也是让我改变看法的关键点：我还发现——也许这不是放之四海而皆准，但至少在 37signals、就我自己的经验来说——我现在做程序员的快乐，比起自从 2000 年代初我刚发现 Ruby 以来的任何时候都更多。

speaker: Guest
text_en: This has the, I just discovered Ruby feel to it, that it is so satisfying to be able to move this fast on so many levels at the same time, to be able to explore the P1s, to be able to think about dual booting Amachi, to do all of that stuff that the work itself has gotten vastly more enjoyable.
text: 这给我的感觉就像“我刚发现 Ruby”那会儿一样：能在这么多层面上同时推进得这么快，真的特别过瘾；能去探索 P1，能去思考让 Amachi 双启动，能做所有这些事，工作本身变得好玩太多了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And I've seen the same thing for the most AI forward programmers that we have. Maybe they also have some of these anxieties, but they're kind of pushed to the side just out of sheer enjoyment working with the new capacities.
text: 而且我也在我们这里那些最积极拥抱 AI 的程序员身上看到了同样的情况。也许他们也有一些这样的焦虑，但因为和这些新能力一起工作实在太有意思了，那些焦虑某种程度上就被挤到一边去了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So there is a bifurcation here where we should all feel like. Well, we don't know what's going on, and for some people, that's going to produce some degree of anxiety.
text: 所以这里确实出现了分化，我们都应该有一种感觉：嗯，我们其实不知道接下来会发生什么，而对有些人来说，这肯定会带来某种程度的焦虑。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I understand that, especially when it's your livelihood and you're like, well, I'd also like to be able to pay for my kid's college in seven years. What does that look like? I get it.
text: 我能理解，尤其当这关系到你的生计时，你会想：那我七年后还想供得起孩子上大学啊。那到时候会是什么样？我懂。

speaker: Guest
text_en: You're not going to be able to manifest that anxiety into anything productive unless you just plow it into leaning in, right?
text: 你没法把那种焦虑变成任何有成效的东西，除非你把它一股脑投入进去，真正上手，对吧？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Because if you just sit and spin around and try to think about what the world's going to look like seven years from now, you're wasting your time. So that's the only path.
text: 因为如果你只是坐在那里空转，想着七年后的世界会是什么样，那就是在浪费时间。所以这就是唯一的路。

speaker: Guest
text_en: The only path is to either get excited about this, which, I don't think takes that much effort.
text: 唯一的路，要么就是对这件事兴奋起来，而我觉得这其实也不用费太大劲。

speaker: Guest
text_en: As we said, if you sit down with these models, you pull out one of your hobby projects from the closet that you never finished, and you just give it a try. I don't see how you really like computers and not find that experiment enjoyable.
text: 就像我们说的，你坐下来用用这些模型，把你那个一直没做完、被扔在角落里的业余项目翻出来，直接试一试。如果你真的喜欢电脑，我很难想象你会觉得这种实验不好玩。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And I've seen this with people who are getting into it. Kent Beck is such a great example.
text: 而且我已经在一些刚开始接触这东西的人身上看到这种情况了。Kent Beck 就是个特别好的例子。

speaker: Guest
text_en: He's been programming for 52 years, and he is saying, like, he is, he loves doing it, and he found this balance between using the agents to build something ambitious that he always wanted to build.
text: 他已经写了 52 年程序了，而他现在会说，他真的很喜欢做这个，而且他找到了一个平衡：用 agents 去做一个他一直想做的、有野心的项目。

speaker: Guest
text_en: He's building a small talk server, which used to take forever, and now it's getting closer. And it's still taking a long time. And then in between, he's chilling at his...
text: 他在做一个 Smalltalk server，这东西以前要花特别久，现在已经越来越接近做成了。当然，还是得花很长时间。然后中间他就在……

speaker: Guest
text_en: He has his house on the lake, and he just goes and like, just looks at the birds for two hours and then gets back to it. It's beautiful. Kent is, by the way, one of my all-time heroes.
text: 他家在湖边，他就会出去待着，比如看看鸟，一看就是两个小时，然后再回来继续做。特别美好。顺便说一句，Kent 一直都是我心目中的英雄之一。

speaker: Guest
text_en: This was right when I got started in programming, right when I, before I was picking up Ruby, I saw Kent speak at a Danish conference in 2001 on stage, and I was completely mesmerized by his command of both the material, how bold he was, and how great of a speaker he was.
text: 这正好是我刚开始学编程的时候，就在我开始接触 Ruby 之前，我在 2001 年的一场丹麦会议上看到 Kent 上台演讲。我完全被他震住了——不管是他对内容的掌控、他的大胆风格，还是他出色的演讲能力。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And this was after having read Extreme Programming and many of these other things.
text: 而且那时候我已经读过《Extreme Programming》和很多类似的东西了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Smalltalk Best Practices is my number one recommendation for any programmer who wants to learn the nitty-gritty of how to structure a method and a class and the rest of it.
text: 对于任何想学清楚方法、类以及其他这些东西到底该怎么组织的程序员来说，《Smalltalk Best Practices》是我心中的头号推荐。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Smalltalk Best Practices, which is Kent's book from 95, I think, or 96, is to this day my favorite book of all time on tactical programming patterns. So it's wonderful to hear him being agent-pilled while also enjoying the birds.
text: 《Smalltalk Best Practices》是 Kent 在 95 年，我想也可能是 96 年写的书，直到今天它依然是我最喜欢的一本讲战术层面编程模式的书。所以听到他一边被 agent-pilled，一边又享受着鸟叫，真的很棒。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I mean, I try to do that too. And this is actually, there's a bit of a tension right now, is that most of the people I find who are all in, they're working harder than they ever have. And I've seen that with myself now too.
text: 我是说，我自己也会尽量这么做。不过这里面现在确实有点张力：我发现那些真正全情投入的人，比以往任何时候都更拼。我现在在自己身上也看到了这一点。

speaker: Guest
text_en: When you can be this effective and impactful on an hour of supervision of these agents, it's really intoxicating. If you have an active dopamine loop up there that gets triggered when something is shipped, it is just hyperactive right now.
text: 当你只花一个小时监督这些 agents，就能产生这么高的效率和这么大的影响时，那种感觉真的很让人上头。如果你脑子里本来就有一个“东西一发布就会被触发”的多巴胺回路，那它现在简直是过度活跃。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And I need to go, do you know what? This is not like a limited sale. Like, AI is going to be here next month, and the month after that.
text: 然后我就得提醒自己，你知道吗？这又不是什么限时抢购。AI 下个月还会在，下下个月也还会在。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Like, I cannot just operate as though it is a limited sale and I need to get all the dopamine in harvested within the next two weeks.
text: 我不能把自己搞得像是在面对限时促销一样，觉得必须在接下来两周内把所有多巴胺都收割完。

speaker: Guest
text_en: That, I actually think, is the main challenge right now for the people who are furthest along and most pilled on it, is like, remember that this is as bad as they're ever going to be, as the cliche goes, right?
text: 我其实觉得，这就是现在那些走得最前、最吃这套的人面临的主要挑战。就是说，得记住一句老生常谈：现在已经是它们最差的时候了，对吧？

speaker: Guest
text_en: You damn well better find a way not to get consumed entirely about it as exciting as it is. And yeah, there's this consuming is a big deal.
text: 不管这事有多让人兴奋，你最好还是得想办法别让自己完全被它吞没。对，这种“被吞没”的状态影响很大。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Like, with Steve Yagy, he was, he looks a bit more drained than, like, you can see it on the video, but he, he is, he's honest. Like, he's being pulled into this. He's doing, he has friends who are, and when you're on the edge, you're there.
text: 比如 Steve Yagy，他现在就是，你从视频里都能看出来，他整个人比平时更疲惫一点。但他也很坦诚，就是他确实被这股浪潮卷进去了。他身边也有朋友是这样，而当你站在最前沿时，你就会被卷进去。

speaker: Host
text_en: You've clearly been AI-pilled, but how are you finding of keeping a balance of like, all right, stepping away, you know, like I, I know you've, I think you previously talked about the importance of sleep. Apparently you don't have an alarm. Correct.
text: 你显然已经很吃 AI 这套了，但你是怎么保持平衡的？比如说，好，适时抽离一下。像我知道你之前好像提过睡眠的重要性。而且听说你不用闹钟，是吧？

speaker: Guest
text_en: I don't use an alarm, although my wife now does because the kids need to go to school on a regular basis. But yeah, for me, eight hours a night is the best investment you can make in your own cognitive capacity.
text: 我不用闹钟，不过我妻子现在会用，因为孩子们得按时上学。但对我来说，每晚睡满八小时，是你能为自己的认知能力做的最好投资。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So I just am reminded every single time I do not get eight hours that it is such a poor trade. If you go from the eight to the six, I go like, well, I'm going to be awake for, in that case, 18 hours.
text: 所以每次只要我没睡满八小时，我都会再次意识到，这笔交换有多不划算。你要是从八小时砍到六小时，我就会想，好，那样的话我会清醒着过 18 个小时。

speaker: Guest
text_en: What is the drag I'm going to carry for all of those 18 hours for getting one more hour, two more hours by cutting back on the sleep. It is such a bad piece of math. It makes no sense. Now, occasionally it's involuntary.
text: 为了少睡一两个小时、多挤出那一点时间，我接下来这 18 个小时到底要背着多大的负担？这笔账实在太不划算了，根本说不通。当然，偶尔也不是我自己能控制的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I have actually had, especially around this AI stuff, I've had a couple of times, very rare, I can count on two hands the number of times where I've been sleepless, like the brain racing a little too much.
text: 其实我确实有过，尤其是最近这些 AI 相关的事上，我有过几次——非常少，两只手都数得过来——会失眠，就是脑子转得太快，停不下来。

speaker: Guest
text_en: That's not typical for me, and it's still not typical, but I have had a couple of them, right? So I get where some of that excitement comes from, but I'd also say the last thing you should trade is sleep and then you should not trade your health.
text: 这对我来说并不常见，现在也依然不常见，不过我确实经历过几次，对吧？所以我能理解那种兴奋感有一部分是从哪来的。但我也想说，你最不该拿去交换的就是睡眠，其次也不该拿健康去换。

speaker: Guest
text_en: You should not try to save the three hours a week of working out to do more agent work. That's a very poor trade. Keep in good condition.
text: 你不该为了多做一点 agent 的工作，就把每周锻炼的那三小时省下来。这是笔很糟糕的交易。把身体状态保持好。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Like, there's nothing that's going to be more important if you want to keep, like, sharp up there that, like, the rest of the system is operating, if not a peak capacity, then at a good sustainable level, right?
text: 如果你想让脑子一直保持敏锐，那没有什么比整个身体系统都在正常运转更重要了。就算达不到峰值状态，至少也要维持在一个良好、可持续的水平，对吧？

speaker: Guest
text_en: And I do think there are some individuals right now who are at fear of running ragged on some. I think that we're going to be dealing with for like, slow down, buddy. Like, it's not, again, a limited sale.
text: 而且我确实觉得，现在有些人已经快把自己折腾垮了。对这种情况，我觉得我们得说一句：慢点，兄弟。这个东西说到底又不是什么限时抢购。

speaker: Guest
text_en: The next 10 years, we're going to see more and more. It's going to get crazier and crazier. So don't squander your health. Don't squander your sleep.
text: 接下来的 10 年，我们只会看到越来越多，这一切会变得越来越疯狂。所以别挥霍你的健康，别挥霍你的睡眠。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Don't squander your diet in the service of anything because even on the short term, it does not work.
text: 也别为了任何事情牺牲你的饮食，因为哪怕只看短期，这么做也没用。

speaker: Guest
text_en: You cannot get more productive within three weeks, let's say, by trying to cut back two or three hours of sleep every night and then think there's anything coherent left after three weeks. You will be a hot mess. So let's close.
text: 比如说，你不可能靠每晚少睡两三个小时，在三周内变得更高效，然后还指望三周之后脑子里还能剩下什么清晰连贯的东西。你会彻底乱成一团。那我们就收个尾吧。

speaker: Host
text_en: We talked about the stuff that we don't know, a lot of things we don't know, but let's close with what you do know. So you could have retired a long time ago and just, you know, kicked back and like listened to birds.
text: 我们聊了很多我们不知道的东西，确实有很多事我们还不知道。但最后我们来聊聊你知道的。你其实很早以前就可以退休了，随便放松一下，听听鸟叫之类的。

speaker: Host
text_en: What is it that keeps you doing, keeps you building, keeping getting up every day and before AI, you would open your terminal. I think you shared like, like you would go and write. Now you're doing with agents. Like what drives you?
text: 到底是什么让你一直做下去、一直创造、每天都起床投入其中？在 AI 出现之前，你会打开终端。我记得你分享过，你会去写代码。现在你是在和 agents 一起做这些。到底是什么在驱动你？

speaker: Guest
text_en: And looking ahead, like what, what are things you're excited about? My drive continues to be a deep love of computers. This is simply the best way, the most fun way to spend my time. I could spend my time on a lot of things.
text: 再往前看，哪些事情会让你感到兴奋？驱动我的，始终是我对计算机深深的热爱。这就是我打发时间最好的方式，也是最有趣的方式。我当然也可以把时间花在很多别的事情上。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I do spend my time on a lot of things. I don't just do computers. I drive race cars. I take lots of time off. I have three kids. We enjoy all of that stuff. But if I'm going to fill eight hours every day with an activity, my best bet is computers.
text: 我确实也会把时间花在很多事情上。我不只是玩计算机。我会开赛车，也会给自己放很多假，我还有三个孩子，我们也很享受这些事情。但如果我每天要拿出八个小时来做一件事，那最适合我的还是计算机。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And it has been so since I was literally five years old, whether it's video games or what now feels a little bit like a video game, actually instrumenting all these agents and playing a little bit of Starcraft with moving them around and yes, exactly.
text: 而且从我真的只有五岁起就是这样了。不管是电子游戏，还是现在这种某种程度上也有点像电子游戏的事——去编排这些 agents，像打一点 Starcraft 那样调动它们，对，没错。

speaker: Guest
text_en: So I just really like computers. So whether I need to do so for economic reasons or not, I will continue to play with computers, see what makes them tick and make things.
text: 所以我就是单纯真的很喜欢计算机。所以不管我是不是出于经济原因必须这么做，我都会继续折腾计算机，研究它们到底是怎么运作的，然后做出东西来。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I think that's the other big misconception that some people have about wealth is that they conceive of it as some sort of checkpoint. Like once you've made it, then you can just kick back and leisure as though that was happiness.
text: 我觉得大家对财富还有一个很大的误解，就是有些人会把它想成某种检查点。好像一旦你成功了，就可以往后一躺，开始享清福，仿佛那就是幸福。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We simply have a hundred years of psychological studies telling us, no, that's misery. If you have all the time in the world and no purpose, no mission, leisure is not going to cut it, is not going to be fulfilling way.
text: 但过去一百年的心理学研究其实已经很明确地告诉我们：不是，那样反而很痛苦。如果你拥有全世界的时间，却没有目标、没有使命感，光靠休闲是远远不够的，也不会让你真正满足。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And this should be obvious by example of literally every entrepreneur who sells their business. They sit on the beach for three weeks and then they're back into the game, right?
text: 这一点其实看看那些把公司卖掉的创业者就很明显了。他们在海滩上坐个三周，然后就又回到赛场了，对吧？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Because this is actually not just something they do in pursuit of a goal. It's the goal itself. It is the mission itself. It is the satisfaction. It is the affirmation of being a human that I'm not just a blob laying around.
text: 因为这其实不只是他们为了实现某个目标而做的事。它本身就是目标。它本身就是使命。它就是满足感。它是在确认，作为一个人，我不只是瘫在那儿的一团东西。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I am a useful individual who put my skills to the best use possible. So I'm going to continue to do that.
text: 我是一个有用的人，我把自己的技能尽可能发挥到了最好。所以我会继续这么做。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And I'm going to continue to do it whether I'm sitting tapping at the keyboard, whether I'm instrumenting these agents, whether they're teaching me, however, which way it is, I want to play with computers. I want to continue to do that.
text: 而且不管我是坐在那里敲键盘，还是在给这些 agents 做配置和接入，还是它们在教我，不管具体是哪种方式，我都想和电脑打交道。我想继续做这件事。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And then even more specifically, after the last three months, I'm leaning in hard now with agent accessibility. For example, this is what I've been doing the last few weeks.
text: 再更具体一点，经过过去三个月之后，我现在非常投入在 agent 可访问性这件事上。比如说，这就是我过去几周一直在做的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We've been working on the new CLI, which also taught me like we're not quite at AGI yet, right? You think like, well, just ask the agent to make a CLI. It will. But like, it's not quite there, right? Like I want it to be just right.
text: 我们一直在做新的 CLI，这也让我意识到，我们离 AGI 其实还没到，对吧？你会觉得，嗯，直接让 agent 去做一个 CLI 不就行了。它是能做，但还没完全到那个程度，对吧？因为我希望它做得恰到好处。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And the agents still need a little bit of help. I'm very happy to provide that help to these agents and we'll release a great CLI for Basecamp very, very shortly. Maybe by the time this is out, it'll probably be out. And for the rest of them too.
text: 而且这些 agents 还是需要一点帮助。我非常乐意给它们提供这种帮助。我们很快就会为 Basecamp 发布一个非常非常棒的 CLI。也许等这期内容播出的时候，它大概已经上线了。其他产品也会有。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And I want to lean into all of this. How can we use this as much as we possibly can? And then right now I'm also just an incredibly curious person. I wake up every morning. I have a new ritual, which is not to pull my phone up and start hopping on X.
text: 而我想更深入地投入这一切。我们怎样才能尽可能多地利用这些东西？另外，我现在也真的是一个好奇心爆棚的人。我每天早上醒来，都有了一个新的仪式，那就是不去拿起手机，不是一醒来就开始刷 X。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Like right when I wake up, I don't think actually that is great. But it takes a tremendous willpower to not do so because I'm just so curious about what happened. There's so much happening right now. I want to know. I want to know.
text: 就刚醒的时候，我其实觉得那样并不好。但要忍住不这么做，需要非常强的意志力，因为我实在太好奇到底发生了什么。现在有太多事情在发生了。我想知道，我想知道。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I want to be enjoying it, be a part of it. So I don't foresee that ending. I don't foresee a love of computers evaporating. In fact, if anything, right now, I'm seeing like a flourishing of it. I'm liking computers more than I did five years ago.
text: 我想享受这件事，想参与其中。所以我不觉得这种状态会结束。我也不觉得自己对电脑的热爱会消退。真要说的话，反而是现在这种热爱更旺盛了。我现在比五年前更喜欢电脑了。

speaker: Host
text_en: And that's amazing. Amazing. David, this was awesome. Thanks. Thanks a bunch. All right. Thanks for having me. This is really great. This was a fascinating conversation and I love the energy that David has.
text: 这太棒了，真的太棒了。David，这期聊得太精彩了。谢谢，太感谢了。好，感谢你邀请我来。这真的很棒。这是一场很有意思的对话，我也很喜欢 David 身上的那股能量。

speaker: Host
text_en: I hope some of this energy that is obvious in person also came across to you. I really appreciated that David was open that his stance did not change about AI because his philosophy changed.
text: 我希望这种当面就能明显感受到的能量，你们也能从节目里感受到。我也很欣赏 David 很坦诚地说，他对 AI 的立场并不是因为自己的理念变了才改变的。

speaker: Host
text_en: It's just that the tools became good enough to do useful stuff. AI for autocomplete was annoying for experienced developers. AI agents that can produce pretty good working code by themselves, on the other hand, are now pretty useful.
text: 而只是因为这些工具终于好到足以做一些真正有用的事了。用于自动补全的 AI 对有经验的开发者来说很烦人。可另一边，能够自己产出相当不错、能跑起来的代码的 AI agents，现在就已经很有用了。

speaker: Host
text_en: And yet David kept coming back to taste, judgment and craft. He wasn't just saying, just let the model write whatever. It's the opposite. He has a very high quality bar and he wants the output to be code that he would actually be proud to merge.
text: 但即便如此，David 还是不断回到品味、判断力和手艺这些点上。他并不是在说，直接让模型随便写就行。恰恰相反。他对质量的要求非常高，他希望产出的代码是那种自己真的会很自豪地合并进去的代码。

speaker: Host
text_en: It feels like AI might make good judgment even more valuable than before. I also really liked how David thinks about the importance of design.
text: 感觉 AI 可能会让优秀的判断力变得比以前更有价值。我也很喜欢 David 对设计重要性的看法。

speaker: Guest
text_en: At 37signals, designers help figure out what should be built, how it should work, and increasingly even decide how it gets implemented.
text: 在 37signals，设计师会参与判断应该做什么、产品该怎么运作，而且越来越多地，他们甚至还会决定具体该怎么实现。

speaker: Host
text_en: I wonder if 37signals is a step ahead of the industry in thinking about designers a bit like developers as well and developers a bit like designers as well.
text: 我在想，37signals 是不是在这件事上比整个行业都更领先一步：他们会把设计师也看得有点像开发者，同时也把开发者看得有点像设计师。

speaker: Host
text_en: Finally, I found David's take that we might have hit peak software engineer an interesting argument. David thinks we'll produce more software than ever.
text: 最后，我觉得 David 关于我们可能已经到了软件工程师巅峰期这个看法，是个很有意思的观点。David 认为，我们产出的软件会比以往任何时候都更多。

speaker: Host
text_en: But his observation is that we might be nearing the end of the time when developers could command high compensation simply because they were the bottleneck.
text: 但他的观察是，我们可能正在接近这样一个时代的尾声：开发者仅仅因为自己是瓶颈，就能拿到高薪。

speaker: Host
text_en: My two cents is that there will surely be high demand for professionals who can build profitable software, but this will mean software engineers who are not only good at coding or using AI to generate code, but can oversee building complex systems, have taste, and business sense as well.
text: 我的一点看法是，市场肯定还是会非常需要那些能做出赚钱软件的专业人士，但这意味着，软件工程师不能只是会写代码，或者会用 AI 生成代码，还得能统筹构建复杂系统，有审美，也有商业判断。

speaker: Host
text_en: If you'd like to hear more from David, check out a bonus episode with him linked in the show notes. Also check out the show notes for related to the pragmatic engineer, deep dives on software craftsmanship, and practical ways of building software.
text: 如果你还想听 David 的更多分享，可以去听一集和他有关的加更内容，链接就在节目说明里。也别忘了看看节目说明，里面还有和 pragmatic engineer 相关的内容、关于软件工艺的深度解析，以及一些构建软件的实用方法。

speaker: Host
text_en: If you enjoyed this podcast, please do subscribe on your favorite podcast platform and on YouTube. And a special thank you if you also leave a rating on the show. Thanks and see you in the next one.
text: 如果你喜欢这期播客，欢迎在你常用的播客平台和 YouTube 上订阅。要是你愿意顺手给节目打个分，那就更感谢了。谢谢，我们下期见。
